groups.description: Spot the rule behind a sequence of numbers, letters or figures and continue it.

topics.description: Find the missing or next number by spotting the pattern.

videos.label: Number Series Reasoning Tricks — the easy way
videos.note: Arithmetic, geometric, square/cube & mixed patterns with examples

understand.realLife: Odometer readings, EMI schedules, a staircase where each step rises the same height — numbers around us often follow a rule. A number series gives you a few terms of such a rule and asks for the next one (or a missing one). The whole skill is detective work: figure out what is being done to get from one number to the next.
understand.coreIdea: You are shown a row of numbers that follow a hidden rule and asked to continue it. The rule is usually simple: add the same amount each time, multiply by the same amount, square/cube the counting numbers, or two rules taking turns. Your job is to test these common patterns quickly and spot the one that fits.
understand.howToThink: Start by finding the difference between consecutive terms. If the difference is constant, it is an arithmetic pattern (add a fixed number). If each term is a fixed multiple of the previous, it is a geometric pattern (multiply). If the differences themselves grow steadily (2, 4, 6, …), look at squares/cubes or a 'difference of differences'. If nothing fits, check whether two separate series are interleaved (alternate terms), or whether the rule is 'multiply then add'.

patterns.name: Arithmetic (constant difference)
patterns.howItWorks: Each term is the one before plus a fixed number d. Spot it when consecutive differences are equal.

patterns.name: Geometric (constant ratio)
patterns.howItWorks: Each term is the previous times a fixed number r. Spot it when each term ÷ the last is the same.

patterns.name: Squares / cubes
patterns.howItWorks: Terms are the counting numbers squared or cubed; useful when differences grow as 3, 5, 7, …

patterns.name: Growing difference
patterns.howItWorks: The gaps change in a regular way (e.g. +2, +4, +6). Add the next gap to get the term.

patterns.name: Interleaved / mixed
patterns.whatItLooksLike: two rules alternate, or 'multiply then add'
patterns.howItWorks: Odd and even positions follow separate rules, or each step both multiplies and adds.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Arithmetic series
workedTypes.idea: Constant difference. What makes it different: subtract neighbours to find d.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: 5, 9, 13, 17, ?

steps.why: Equal differences mean 'add d each time'.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Geometric series
workedTypes.idea: Constant ratio. What makes it different: divide neighbours to find r.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: 3, 6, 12, 24, ?

steps.why: A constant ratio means 'multiply by r each time'.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Squares or cubes
workedTypes.idea: Terms are n² or n³. What makes it different: recognise perfect squares/cubes.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: 1, 4, 9, 16, ?

steps.why: Recognising perfect squares reveals the rule.
Continue with the next counting number squared.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Growing difference
workedTypes.idea: The gaps themselves form a pattern. What makes it different: look at the second row of differences.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: 2, 3, 5, 8, 12, ?

steps.action: Gaps: 1, 2, 3, 4 — increasing by 1 each time; next gap is 5.
steps.why: The differences follow their own simple rule.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Mixed rule (multiply then add)
workedTypes.idea: Each step both multiplies and adds. What makes it different: test ×k + c.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: 2, 5, 11, 23, ?

steps.why: When plain add/multiply fails, try a combined rule.
Apply the same rule once more.

traps: Guessing the rule from just two terms — always confirm it across at least three gaps.
Only checking addition; many series are multiplicative or 'multiply then add'.
Missing an interleaved pattern — try reading alternate terms as two separate series.
Overlooking perfect squares/cubes when the differences grow as 3, 5, 7, ….
Arithmetic slips (a wrong subtraction) that make a clean pattern look broken.
understand.memoryHook: Difference first: equal → add; if not, ratio → multiply; if not, think squares/cubes; still stuck → alternate terms or 'multiply then add'.

crack.oneLine: Always take differences first — constant means add, a constant ratio means multiply, growing gaps mean squares/cubes or a difference pattern, and if all fail try alternate terms or '×k + c'.

techniques.name: Difference test
techniques.method: Write the gaps between terms.

techniques.name: Ratio test
techniques.method: Divide each term by the previous.

techniques.name: Square/cube radar
techniques.method: Compare terms with 1,4,9,16,25 and 1,8,27,64.
techniques.timeSaved: Instant when the pattern is a power.

techniques.name: Alternate-terms split
techniques.method: Read 1st,3rd,5th… and 2nd,4th,6th… as two series.

clueToMethod.clue: Each term a fixed multiple of the last
Gaps form their own simple pattern

clueToMethod.method: Try 'multiply then add' or split alternate terms.

crack.whyItWorks: Every series is generated by one repeatable operation. Differences expose addition, ratios expose multiplication, and second-level differences expose powers — so testing them in that order zeroes in on the rule with the least effort.
bestFor: Standard arithmetic, geometric, square/cube and simple mixed series.
Series where reading alternate terms reveals two clean sub-series.
notFor: Some tough series combine several rules — confirm across three or more terms before committing.
Prime-number or oddly designed series may not fit the usual tests; recognise primes (2,3,5,7,11,…) separately.
crack.practicePattern: Any 'find the next / missing number' is solved by the same ladder: differences → ratio → squares/cubes → gap-pattern → alternate terms / multiply-then-add.

topics.description: Crack alphabet-based sequences and find the missing letters.

videos.label: Letter Series — every pattern type explained
videos.note: Aptitude Made Easy — all pattern types with solved examples

understand.realLife: A letter series is a number series wearing a disguise. Instead of digits you see letters, but each letter has a fixed position in the alphabet (A = 1, B = 2, …, Z = 26). Swap the letters for their positions and the hidden rule appears — usually 'jump forward a fixed number of letters'.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a run of letters that follow a rule and asked for the next one (or a missing one). The rule is almost always about POSITION: move forward (or back) by the same number of steps each time, or by a growing number of steps. Once you write each letter's position number, it becomes an ordinary number series.
understand.howToThink: Memorise the alphabet positions, especially the milestones E(5), J(10), O(15), T(20), Y(25) — the word 'EJOTY' helps. To find a letter's step, subtract positions. Wrapping past Z goes back to A (position 27 → A). Watch for reverse alphabet (A↔Z, B↔Y, where a letter and its mirror add to 27), interleaved two-letter series, and letter+number combinations.

patterns.name: Position value
patterns.howItWorks: Every letter has a fixed number; convert letters to numbers to see the pattern.

patterns.name: EJOTY milestones
patterns.howItWorks: These evenly spaced anchors let you find any position fast (e.g. Q is two after O → 17).

patterns.name: Constant step
patterns.howItWorks: Move forward k letters each time; the commonest rule.
patterns.example: A, D, G, J (+3) → next M.

patterns.name: Reverse-alphabet mirror
patterns.howItWorks: Some series count backwards or pair a letter with its opposite end.
patterns.example: The mirror of E(5) is V(22), since 5 + 22 = 27.

patterns.name: Interleaved / combined
patterns.whatItLooksLike: two letter-series alternate, or letters pair with numbers
patterns.howItWorks: Odd and even positions follow separate steps, or each item is a letter plus a number.
patterns.example: A1, C2, E3, G4 → letters +2, numbers +1.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Constant forward step
workedTypes.idea: Move a fixed number of letters each time. What makes it different: find the step in positions.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next letter: A, C, E, G, ?

steps.why: Converting to numbers shows a constant step of 2.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Growing step
workedTypes.idea: The jump increases each time. What makes it different: the step is not constant.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next letter: A, B, D, G, ?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Reverse (backward) series
workedTypes.idea: Letters move backward. What makes it different: subtract positions.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next letter: Z, X, V, T, ?

steps.why: A backward series subtracts a constant.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Interleaved two series
workedTypes.idea: Two alphabet series take turns. What makes it different: split odd and even positions.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next letter: A, Z, B, Y, C, ?

steps.action: Odd positions A, B, C go forward; even positions Z, Y go backward.
The next term continues the backward series after Y → X.
steps.why: Z, Y, X … steps back by 1.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Letter + number combination
workedTypes.idea: Each item pairs a letter with a number. What makes it different: track both parts.
workedTypes.problem: Find the next term: B2, D4, F6, H8, ?

steps.action: Letters B, D, F, H step by 2; numbers 2, 4, 6, 8 step by 2.
steps.why: Handle the letter and the number as two parallel patterns.

steps.action: Next letter = J (after H), next number = 10 → J10.

traps: Not converting letters to position numbers — the pattern is far harder to see in letters.
Miscounting positions; keep EJOTY (5,10,15,20,25) handy as checkpoints.
Forgetting the wrap: after Z you return to A (treat Z+1 as A).
Missing an interleaved pattern — always test alternate letters as two series.
In letter+number items, updating only the letter and forgetting the number (or vice versa).
understand.memoryHook: Turn letters into numbers (EJOTY = 5,10,15,20,25), then it is just a number series. After Z, wrap to A; for backward series, a letter and its mirror add to 27.

crack.oneLine: Convert letters to positions (lean on EJOTY = 5,10,15,20,25), solve it as a number series, wrap past Z back to A, and split alternate letters when a single rule won't fit.

techniques.name: EJOTY lookup
techniques.method: Anchor on E5, J10, O15, T20, Y25 and count a step or two.

techniques.name: Numberise then difference
techniques.method: Write positions, then take differences as in a number series.

techniques.name: Mirror rule
techniques.method: A letter and its reverse add to 27.

techniques.name: Split alternate
techniques.method: Read 1st,3rd,5th… and 2nd,4th,6th… separately.

clueToMethod.clue: Letters climb by the same amount

clueToMethod.method: Track the letter and number separately.

crack.whyItWorks: Letters are just labels for the numbers 1–26, so every letter-series is a number-series in disguise. The EJOTY anchors and the 'adds to 27' mirror turn slow alphabet counting into quick arithmetic.
bestFor: Position-based series with a constant or growing step, forward or backward.
Interleaved and letter+number series, once you split the parts.
notFor: Skip-based patterns that also drop or repeat letters need careful position tracking, not just a step.
Watch the wrap around Z/A, which can make a step look broken if ignored.
crack.practicePattern: Every letter-series question: convert to positions, find the step (or split alternates), then convert the answer position back to a letter.

topics.description: Find the next figure in a visual sequence.

understand.realLife: The second hand of a clock turns the same angle every tick; a rangoli adds one more ring of dots each round. Figure series work the same way: a shape changes by a fixed rule from one picture to the next, and you predict what comes next. Even when a figure is described in words rather than drawn, the reasoning is identical — find the one thing that changes step by step.
understand.coreIdea: You are shown a sequence of figures and must pick the next one. The change is usually one of a few kinds: the figure ROTATES by a fixed angle, something is ADDED or REMOVED each step (a side, a dot, a line), a marker MOVES to a new position, or two features change together. Describe each figure in words (its shape, how many elements, where the marker points) and the rule pops out.
understand.howToThink: Ask three questions in order: (1) Is it rotating? A quarter-turn is 90°, so after four steps it returns to start — track the angle. (2) Is a count changing? Count sides, dots or lines; look for +1, +2 or ×2. (3) Is a position changing? A marker often moves one corner clockwise each step (4 corners → repeats every 4). If a single rule doesn't fit, two things may be changing at once (e.g. rotate AND add a dot).

patterns.name: Rotation
patterns.whatItLooksLike: each step turns by a fixed angle (often 45° or 90°)
patterns.howItWorks: The figure spins the same amount every time; 90° per step returns to start after 4 steps, 45° after 8.
patterns.example: An arrow turning 90° clockwise: up → right → down → left.

patterns.name: Element count
patterns.whatItLooksLike: number of sides/dots/lines changes by a fixed rule
patterns.howItWorks: Count the elements in each figure; the count grows (or shrinks) by a constant, or doubles.

patterns.name: Position shift
patterns.whatItLooksLike: a marker moves a fixed number of positions each step
patterns.howItWorks: A shaded corner or dot moves, often one step clockwise; with 4 corners it repeats every 4 steps.
patterns.example: Shaded corner: top-left → top-right → bottom-right → bottom-left.

patterns.name: Alternation
patterns.howItWorks: Odd steps look one way, even steps another; the answer continues the alternation.
patterns.example: Circle, square, circle, square → next circle.

patterns.name: Combined change
patterns.howItWorks: The figure may rotate AND gain an element at the same time; track each feature separately.
patterns.example: Rotate 90° and add one dot each step.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Rotation
workedTypes.idea: Figure turns a fixed angle. What makes it different: track the direction it points.
workedTypes.problem: An arrow points up, then right, then down. Which way does it point next?

steps.action: Each step is a 90° clockwise turn: up → right → down.
steps.why: Equal turns mean a constant rotation.

steps.action: Next after down (turning 90° clockwise) is left.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Increasing element count
workedTypes.idea: Something is added each step. What makes it different: count the elements.
workedTypes.problem: The figures contain 1, 2, 3 and 4 dots in order. How many dots in the next figure?

steps.action: The count goes up by 1 each time: 1, 2, 3, 4.
steps.why: A constant increase in the count is the rule.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Shape with a growing number of sides
workedTypes.idea: The polygon gains a side each step. What makes it different: identify the shape by side count.
workedTypes.problem: Triangle, square, pentagon, … what is the next shape?

steps.why: Each figure has one more side than the last.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Moving marker
workedTypes.idea: A shaded corner moves each step. What makes it different: follow the position, not the shape.
workedTypes.problem: A shaded corner of a square moves: top-left, top-right, bottom-right, … where next?

steps.action: The shade moves one corner clockwise each step.
steps.why: A constant positional move is the rule.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Combined rotation and addition
workedTypes.idea: Two changes at once. What makes it different: track each feature separately.
workedTypes.problem: Each step the figure rotates 90° clockwise AND gains one line. It starts with 1 line pointing up. After 2 steps, how many lines and which way is the original line pointing?

steps.action: Lines: 1 → 2 → 3 (add one per step). Direction: up → right → down (90° each step).
After 2 steps: 3 lines, original pointing down.

traps: Trying to see everything at once — isolate ONE feature (rotation, count, or position) at a time.
Confusing clockwise and anticlockwise; fix the direction from the first two figures.
Forgetting rotation repeats: 90° per step returns to start after 4 steps.
Missing a second change — many figures rotate AND add/remove an element together.
Miscounting elements; recount dots/sides/lines carefully for each figure.
understand.memoryHook: Ask in order: is it turning (angle?), is a count changing (+1, ×2?), or is a marker moving (one corner clockwise?). If one rule doesn't fit, two things are changing at once.

crack.oneLine: Isolate one changing feature at a time — rotation angle, element count, or marker position — and if a single rule won't fit, track two features together.

techniques.name: Rotation clock
techniques.method: 90° per step repeats every 4 figures; 45° every 8.
techniques.example: Step 5 of a 90° series = same as step 1.

techniques.name: Count the elements
techniques.method: Tally dots/sides/lines per figure and difference them.
techniques.timeSaved: Turns figures into a number series.

techniques.name: Follow the marker
techniques.method: Track only the moving/shaded element's position.

techniques.name: Two-feature split
techniques.method: When stuck, assume two rules and solve each.
techniques.howToApply: e.g. rotate AND add a dot.

drill.question: 90° per step returns to start after how many steps?

crack.whyItWorks: A figure series is generated by repeating a fixed transformation, exactly like a number series. Rotations are periodic (they cycle), counts behave like arithmetic series, and positions cycle through a fixed set — so naming the one repeating operation predicts the next figure.
bestFor: Clear single-feature changes: rotation, growing/shrinking count, or a moving marker.
Combined patterns, once you split the features and track each.
notFor: Highly detailed visual puzzles can hide subtle changes (shading, size) that words may under-describe — read the description carefully.
Some figure sets rely on symmetry or overlap rules that need the actual picture; treat those visually.
crack.practicePattern: Every figure-series question: describe each figure, find the one feature that changes by a fixed rule (or two features together), and continue it.

groups.description: Crack coded words, complete analogies, and pick the odd one out by finding the hidden relationship.

topics.description: Crack the rule that turns words into codes, then decode new ones.

videos.label: Coding & Decoding — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: 5.5M views — every coding type from basic to hard, ~1 hr

understand.realLife: A secret message between friends, a shop's price tag written in code, or a computer swapping letters for numbers — all use a hidden rule to turn ordinary words into a disguised form. Coding-decoding gives you a word and its code, asks you to discover that rule, and then use it to code or decode a brand-new word.
understand.coreIdea: You are shown how one word becomes a code (for example, CAT → DBU). Your task is to find WHAT was done to each letter — usually 'move forward/back a fixed number of letters', 'reverse the word', or 'swap by position'. Once you know the rule, you apply the same rule to whatever the question asks.
understand.howToThink: Give each letter its position (A = 1 … Z = 26). Then compare the code letter with the original: a constant shift like +1 (A→B) or −1 is the commonest rule. Other families are: reversal (write the word backwards, or replace each letter by its mirror where letter + mirror = 27), number coding (letters map to their positions or a sum), and substitution/'fictional language' where whole words map to words. Always crack the rule on the given example first, then apply it.

patterns.name: Letter shift
patterns.howItWorks: Every letter moves forward or back by the same amount k. Wrapping past Z returns to A.

patterns.name: Reverse the word
patterns.whatItLooksLike: write the letters in reverse order
patterns.howItWorks: The code is simply the original spelt backwards.

patterns.name: Mirror letters
patterns.howItWorks: A↔Z, B↔Y, C↔X, …; replace each letter by its partner from the other end.

patterns.name: Number coding
patterns.howItWorks: Replace letters by A=1…Z=26, or add those values for a single number.

patterns.name: Word substitution
patterns.whatItLooksLike: whole words map to other words consistently
patterns.howItWorks: In 'if X means Y' puzzles, track which word stands for which by matching common words across sentences.
patterns.example: 'sky is blue' coded, 'blue is deep' coded → find 'blue'.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Constant letter shift
workedTypes.idea: Each letter moves by a fixed amount. What makes it different: find k from one pair.
workedTypes.problem: If CAT is coded as DBU, how is DOG coded?

steps.why: Comparing positions shows a constant forward shift.

steps.action: Apply +1 to DOG: D→E, O→P, G→H → EPH.
steps.why: Use the same rule on the new word.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Reversal
workedTypes.idea: The code is the word backwards. What makes it different: no shift, just order.
workedTypes.problem: If BOOK is coded as KOOB, how is DESK coded?

steps.action: BOOK reversed is KOOB — the rule is 'reverse the letters'.
steps.why: The letters are the same, only the order flipped.
Apply reversal to the new word.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Mirror (opposite letters)
workedTypes.idea: Each letter becomes its alphabet mirror. What makes it different: use letter + mirror = 27.
workedTypes.problem: If AZ is coded as ZA, how is BY coded?

steps.action: A↔Z and Z↔A are mirrors (positions add to 27).
steps.why: The rule replaces each letter by its opposite end.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Number coding
workedTypes.idea: Letters map to position numbers. What makes it different: convert letters to numbers.
workedTypes.problem: If A = 1 and pattern continues, what is the code for the word ACE?

steps.why: Each letter takes its alphabet position.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Word substitution language
workedTypes.idea: Whole words are swapped consistently. What makes it different: match common words across two coded sentences.
workedTypes.problem: If 'pit dar' means 'red apple' and 'dar tim' means 'apple juice', what does 'dar' mean?

steps.action: Both sentences share the word 'apple' in English and 'dar' in code.
steps.why: The common code word matches the common English word.
It is the only word appearing in both.

traps: Not confirming the shift on every letter of the example — check all letters, not just the first.
Forgetting the wrap: after Z the shift returns to A (and before A back to Z).
Confusing a forward shift with a backward one; note the direction carefully.
Mixing mirror (adds to 27) with a plain shift — they look similar for some letters.
In substitution puzzles, guessing instead of matching the word common to two sentences.
understand.memoryHook: Crack the rule on the GIVEN pair first: check the shift (+k/−k), or reversal, or mirror (adds to 27), or numbers — then apply the very same rule to the asked word.

crack.oneLine: Decode the rule from the example — constant shift, reversal, mirror (adds to 27), or number coding — then apply it unchanged to the new word, minding the Z→A wrap.

techniques.name: Find the shift
techniques.method: Subtract the original letter's position from the code letter's; if constant, that is k.
techniques.howToApply: code − original = k for every letter.

techniques.name: Spot reversal
techniques.method: Check if the code is the word backwards.

techniques.name: Mirror = 27
techniques.method: Replace letter position p by 27 − p.

techniques.name: Common-word match
techniques.method: In substitution puzzles, line up sentences and match the shared word.
techniques.howToApply: Shared code word = shared English word.

crack.whyItWorks: Coding is a fixed transformation applied letter-by-letter (or word-by-word), so once you reverse-engineer it from the known pair, it is guaranteed to behave identically on any new input — reasoning, not memory, gives the answer.
bestFor: Letter-shift, reversal, mirror and position-number codes.
Substitution 'languages' where two sentences share a word.
notFor: Mixed codes (shift some letters, reverse others) need you to test each letter separately.
Codes with irregular or symbol-based rules may not fit the standard families — study the example closely.
crack.practicePattern: Every coding item: find the rule on the given example (shift / reverse / mirror / number / substitution), then apply that exact rule to the word in the question.

topics.description: Complete 'A is to B as C is to ?' by finding the relationship.

understand.realLife: 'A puppy is to a dog as a kitten is to a cat' — you instantly feel why that fits: young-to-adult. Analogies test exactly this sense of relationship. You are given a pair that are linked in some way and must find another pair linked in the SAME way.
understand.coreIdea: An analogy reads 'A is to B as C is to ?'. First work out the precise relationship between A and B (what B is to A), then apply that identical relationship to C to find the missing item. The trick is to name the link in a short sentence — 'a doctor works in a hospital' — and reuse it.
understand.howToThink: Analogies come in families you can learn to spot: synonym/antonym (hot–cold), part-to-whole (wheel–car), worker-to-tool or worker-to-place (chef–kitchen), cause-and-effect (rain–flood), object-to-use (pen–write), and for letters/numbers, position or arithmetic relationships (2:4 as 3:6 is 'double'). Frame the link as a sentence, then test which option makes the same sentence true.

patterns.name: Name the link as a sentence
patterns.whatItLooksLike: 'A is the ___ of B' → apply to C
patterns.howItWorks: State the exact relationship in words, then plug C into the same sentence.
patterns.example: 'Petal is part of a flower' → 'finger is part of a hand'.

patterns.name: Synonym / antonym
patterns.howItWorks: The pair means the same or the exact opposite.

patterns.name: Part to whole
patterns.whatItLooksLike: A is a part of B
patterns.howItWorks: One item is a component of the other.

patterns.name: Worker : tool / place
patterns.howItWorks: A person is linked to their instrument or workplace.

patterns.name: Letter / number relation
patterns.howItWorks: For symbols, look at alphabet positions or a numeric operation.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Synonym / antonym
workedTypes.idea: The pair means the same or opposite. What makes it different: check meaning first.
workedTypes.problem: Hot : Cold :: Up : ?

steps.why: Name the link: it is antonyms.

steps.action: The opposite of Up is Down.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Part to whole
workedTypes.idea: One item is part of the other. What makes it different: identify component vs whole.
workedTypes.problem: Wheel : Car :: Petal : ?

steps.action: A wheel is a part of a car.
A petal is a part of a flower.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Worker and place/tool
workedTypes.idea: Person linked to workplace or instrument. What makes it different: state who-does-what-where.
workedTypes.problem: Teacher : School :: Doctor : ?

steps.action: A teacher works in a school.
A doctor works in a hospital.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Object and use
workedTypes.idea: Item linked to its function. What makes it different: 'used to…'.
workedTypes.problem: Pen : Write :: Knife : ?

steps.action: A pen is used to write.
A knife is used to cut.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Number / letter relationship
workedTypes.idea: Symbols linked by arithmetic or position. What makes it different: find the operation.

traps: Not pinning the relationship precisely — 'related somehow' is too vague; use a full sentence.
Keeping the order loose: 'A is to B' must map to 'C is to ?' in the SAME direction.
Choosing an option that is merely associated rather than matching the exact link.
For number analogies, stopping at the first operation that fits A:B without checking it truly is the pattern.
Ignoring the closest, most specific relationship when two options seem to fit.
understand.memoryHook: Say the link out loud as a sentence — 'A is the ___ of B' — then read C into the same sentence. The option that keeps the sentence true is the answer.

crack.oneLine: Turn the first pair's link into a precise sentence, keep the same direction, and pick the option that makes the identical sentence true for the second pair.

techniques.name: Sentence test
techniques.method: Write 'A is the ___ of B', then substitute C and the option.

techniques.name: Direction check
techniques.method: Keep A→B and C→? in the same order.

techniques.name: Number operation scan
techniques.method: Test +, −, ×, square, cube between A and B.
techniques.howToApply: Apply the same operation to C.

techniques.name: Letter position
techniques.method: Convert letters to positions and find the step.
techniques.howToApply: Same step for the second pair.

clueToMethod.clue: One word is a piece of the other
A thing and what it does

crack.whyItWorks: An analogy is an equation of relationships: if the link A→B is the same as C→?, then '?' is forced. Stating the link as a sentence makes that hidden equation explicit, so the right option is the one that satisfies it.
bestFor: Word pairs with a clear semantic link (part-whole, worker-place, object-use, synonym/antonym).
Number/letter pairs with a single clean operation or position step.
notFor: Watch for two options fitting loosely — choose the most specific, exact relationship.
Some pairs have multiple plausible links; use the answer options to decide which link the setter intends.
crack.practicePattern: Every analogy: name the A–B link precisely, keep the direction, and apply it to C to select the matching option.

topics.description: Group items by a common rule and find the one that doesn't fit.

understand.realLife: Show anyone 'apple, banana, mango, carrot' and they will pick carrot — it is a vegetable among fruits. That instinct is exactly the skill: find the common thread that ties most items together, then spot the single item that breaks it.
understand.coreIdea: You are given four or five items and must find the ODD ONE — the one that does not share the property the others have. The property might be a category (fruits vs a vegetable), a number property (perfect squares vs a non-square), a letter pattern, or a shared feature. Find what the majority have in common; the exception is your answer.
understand.howToThink: Test common bases in turn: (1) Category/meaning — are they all animals, metals, months…? (2) Number property — all even, all prime, all perfect squares, all multiples of something? (3) Letter/position pattern — same number of letters, same vowel count, a fixed alphabet step, or letters that are all consonants? (4) Structural feature — all have four sides, all are leap-relevant, etc. The item failing the shared rule is the odd one.

patterns.name: Find the common rule
patterns.whatItLooksLike: identify the property shared by MOST items
patterns.howItWorks: Look for the single feature that all but one item satisfy.
patterns.example: Rose, Lily, Lotus, Mango → all flowers except Mango.

patterns.name: Number properties
patterns.whatItLooksLike: even/odd, prime, perfect square/cube, multiple of k
patterns.howItWorks: Check whether all-but-one share a numeric property.

patterns.name: Prime vs composite
patterns.howItWorks: Spot the non-prime among primes (or vice versa).

patterns.name: Letter patterns
patterns.whatItLooksLike: letter count, vowels, or a fixed alphabet step
patterns.howItWorks: Group words by a letter-based feature and find the exception.
patterns.example: CAT, DOG, PEN, TIGER → TIGER has 5 letters, others 3.

patterns.name: Category / class
patterns.howItWorks: All belong to one class except one from a different class.
patterns.example: Copper, Iron, Gold, Oxygen → Oxygen is not a metal.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Category odd one
workedTypes.idea: One item is from a different real-world class. What makes it different: name the shared category.
workedTypes.problem: Find the odd one: Apple, Banana, Mango, Potato.

steps.action: Apple, Banana, Mango are fruits; Potato is a vegetable.
So the odd one is Potato.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Perfect squares
workedTypes.idea: All but one are perfect squares. What makes it different: test square-ness.
workedTypes.problem: Find the odd one: 4, 9, 16, 20.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Prime vs non-prime
workedTypes.idea: One number is not prime. What makes it different: count factors.
workedTypes.problem: Find the odd one: 2, 3, 5, 9.

steps.why: It has more than two factors.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Letter count / pattern
workedTypes.idea: One word differs in a letter feature. What makes it different: count letters or vowels.
workedTypes.problem: Find the odd one: CAT, DOG, PEN, TIGER.

steps.action: CAT, DOG, PEN have 3 letters; TIGER has 5.
So the odd one is TIGER.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Number relationship (multiples)
workedTypes.idea: All but one share a divisor. What makes it different: test divisibility.
workedTypes.problem: Find the odd one: 15, 25, 35, 42.

traps: Settling on the first difference you see — check for the property shared by the MOST items.
Overlooking number properties (prime, square, multiple) when items are numbers.
Missing letter-based rules (letter count, vowels, alphabet step) for word groups.
Finding two possible odd ones and not choosing the rule that leaves exactly one exception.
Being distracted by surface similarity instead of the true classifying rule.
understand.memoryHook: Ask 'what do three of these four have in common?' Category, number property, or letter pattern — whichever rule fits three items, the fourth is the odd one out.

crack.oneLine: Find the property shared by MOST items — category, number property (prime/square/multiple), or a letter pattern — and the one item that breaks it is the answer.

techniques.name: Category scan
techniques.method: Ask if they are all animals/fruits/metals/etc.
techniques.howToApply: Name the class covering three items.

techniques.name: Number-property test
techniques.method: Check even/odd, prime, square, multiple of k.
techniques.howToApply: The one failing the shared property is odd.

techniques.name: Letter-feature test
techniques.method: Count letters/vowels or check an alphabet step.
techniques.howToApply: The word breaking the feature is odd.

techniques.name: Majority rule
techniques.method: Pick the rule that leaves exactly ONE exception.
techniques.howToApply: Reject rules that leave two odd items.

clueToMethod.method: Choose the rule leaving exactly one.

drill.question: Odd one: Rose, Lily, Mango, Lotus?
Odd one: Cow, Dog, Lion, Sparrow?

crack.whyItWorks: Classification is analogy's mirror image: instead of matching a relationship, you find the shared property that defines a group. Because a well-formed question leaves exactly one exception, systematically testing category, number and letter rules always isolates it.
bestFor: Clear category sets and numeric sets with a single shared property.
Word sets governed by a letter-count or pattern rule.
notFor: Beware sets with more than one defensible rule — pick the one leaving a single odd item.
Figure/shape classifications may need visual features that words describe only loosely.
crack.practicePattern: Every odd-one-out: find the property three of four items share (category / number / letter), and the item lacking it is the answer.

groups.description: Work out family relationships and track directions and distances.

topics.description: Untangle family trees to find how two people are related.

videos.label: Blood Relations — 5 must-solve practice sets
videos.note: Family-tree method applied to exam-level puzzles

understand.realLife: When someone says 'my mother's only brother's son', you can work out that is your cousin — you just follow the family links one step at a time. Blood-relation questions are exactly this: a chain of relationships that you untangle to name how two people are connected.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a sentence linking people ('A is the father of B, B is the sister of C') and asked how two of them are related. The reliable method is to DRAW a small family tree: put each generation on its own level, mark males and females, and read the answer straight off the picture instead of holding it all in your head.
understand.howToThink: Use simple symbols: '+' or a square for male, '−' or a circle for female; a vertical line for parent-to-child; a horizontal double line for husband-wife; and a single horizontal line for siblings. Keep generations on levels: grandparents on top, parents next, children below. Two special tools: (1) in 'pointing' puzzles ('pointing to a photo, he said…'), start from the speaker and walk the chain; (2) watch gender words — son/daughter, brother/sister, nephew/niece, paternal (father's side) vs maternal (mother's side).

patterns.name: Draw the tree
patterns.howItWorks: Turn every sentence into marks on a family tree; the relation becomes visible.
patterns.example: 'A is father of B' → A□ with a line down to B.

patterns.name: Generation levels
patterns.whatItLooksLike: grandparents / parents / children on separate rows
patterns.howItWorks: Keeping each generation on its own level prevents mixing up uncles with grandparents.

patterns.name: Key relations
patterns.whatItLooksLike: father's/mother's brother = uncle; their child = cousin
patterns.howItWorks: Standard names: parent's sibling = uncle/aunt, their child = cousin, sibling's child = nephew/niece.

patterns.name: Pointing puzzles
patterns.whatItLooksLike: start at the speaker, follow each link
patterns.howItWorks: 'He said, pointing to a photo…' — begin from the person speaking and step through the chain.
patterns.example: 'His father is my father's son' → the man is the speaker's son.

patterns.name: In-law and paternal/maternal
patterns.whatItLooksLike: spouse's family = in-laws; father's side = paternal
patterns.howItWorks: Track marriage links for in-laws and which parent's side for paternal/maternal.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Direct relation
workedTypes.idea: A short chain, name the link. What makes it different: one or two steps.
workedTypes.problem: A is the father of B, and B is the sister of C. How is A related to C?

steps.action: A is parent of B; B and C are siblings, so A is also parent of C.
A is the father of C.
steps.why: A is male and a parent of C.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Two-step (grandparent)
workedTypes.idea: Cross a generation. What makes it different: parent of a parent.
workedTypes.problem: P is the mother of Q, and Q is the mother of R. How is P related to R?

steps.action: P → Q is one generation; Q → R is another.
steps.why: Two parent links stack into a grandparent link.

steps.action: P is the grandmother of R.
steps.why: P is female and two generations above R.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Uncle / cousin
workedTypes.idea: Through a parent's sibling. What makes it different: sideways then down.
workedTypes.problem: X is the brother of Y, and Y is the father of Z. How is X related to Z?

steps.action: X and Y are brothers; Y is Z's father, so X is Z's father's brother.
steps.why: A parent's brother is an uncle.

steps.action: X is the uncle of Z.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Pointing to a photo
workedTypes.idea: Start from the speaker. What makes it different: 'my father's son' type chains.
workedTypes.problem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the man related to the woman?

steps.action: 'My father's only son' is the woman's brother (or the woman's father's son).
steps.why: Begin from the speaker and resolve the innermost phrase.

steps.action: The man is the son of her brother → her nephew.
steps.why: A brother's son is a nephew.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Mixed chain
workedTypes.idea: Several links across genders. What makes it different: draw before answering.
workedTypes.problem: A is the wife of B. B is the son of C. How is C related to A?

steps.action: B is C's son; A is B's wife, so A is C's daughter-in-law.
steps.why: A son's wife is a daughter-in-law.

steps.action: C is the parent-in-law (father- or mother-in-law) of A.

traps: Solving in your head — always draw the tree; it removes almost every error.
Ignoring gender: son vs daughter, uncle vs aunt change the final answer.
Mixing up generations, e.g. calling a grandparent an uncle.
In pointing puzzles, starting from the wrong person — always begin at the speaker.
Confusing paternal (father's side) with maternal (mother's side) relations.
understand.memoryHook: Draw it: males as squares, females as circles, one level per generation. Resolve pointing puzzles from the inside out, starting at the speaker.

crack.oneLine: Draw a quick family tree — squares for men, circles for women, one row per generation — and read the relation straight off it; for pointing puzzles start at the speaker and resolve inner phrases first.

techniques.name: Tree diagram
techniques.method: Convert each sentence into marks on levels.
techniques.howToApply: Parent above, child below, siblings side by side.
techniques.example: 'A father of B' → A above B.
techniques.whenFails: Never — it is the core method.

techniques.name: Inside-out
techniques.method: In 'my father's son' chains, solve the innermost part first.
techniques.howToApply: Resolve 'father's son' before the outer link.

techniques.name: Gender check
techniques.method: Tag each person male/female before naming the relation.
techniques.whenFails: When gender is deliberately hidden (then answer 'cannot be determined').

techniques.name: Generation gap
techniques.method: Count how many parent-links separate the two people.

clueToMethod.method: Start at the speaker, resolve inside-out.

crack.whyItWorks: A family is a graph of parent-child and marriage links. Drawing it makes every path explicit, so naming the relation is just reading the shortest path between two nodes — no memory juggling required.
bestFor: Any chain of stated relationships, however long.
Pointing/photo puzzles, once you start at the speaker.
notFor: If a person's gender is never given, some relations are genuinely undecidable — answer accordingly.
Very large families still need the tree; do not shortcut by memory.
crack.practicePattern: Every blood-relation question: draw the tree from the sentences, tag genders and generations, then read off how the two people connect.

topics.description: Track turns and movements to find final position and distance.

videos.label: Direction Sense — Tricks & Shortcuts (CareerRide)
videos.note: Full concept + turns, distance & shadow problems

understand.realLife: Following directions to a friend's house — 'walk 3 blocks north, turn right, then 4 blocks' — is exactly a direction-sense problem. You track each move on a mental map and, at the end, work out where you are and how far that is from the start.
understand.coreIdea: A person starts at a point and makes a series of moves (so many metres in a direction, then turns). You must find either the final direction from the start, or the straight-line (shortest) distance between start and finish. The trick is to draw the path on graph-like axes and treat the straight-line gap as the hypotenuse of a right triangle.
understand.howToThink: Fix a compass: North up, South down, East right, West left. A 'right turn' rotates you 90° clockwise; a 'left turn' 90° anticlockwise. Add up movements along the North-South axis and along the East-West axis separately. The net East-West gap and net North-South gap are the two legs of a right triangle, so the straight-line distance = √(east-west² + north-south²). Remember the 3-4-5 triangle: legs 3 and 4 give hypotenuse 5.

patterns.name: Compass
patterns.whatItLooksLike: N up, S down, E right, W left
patterns.howItWorks: Draw axes and mark the four directions before you start.
patterns.example: Facing N, East is to your right.

patterns.name: Turns
patterns.howItWorks: Each turn rotates your facing direction by 90°.
patterns.example: Facing N, turn right → face E.

patterns.name: Net displacement
patterns.howItWorks: Add moves along each axis, then take the difference.
patterns.example: Walk 5 E then 2 W → net 3 E.

patterns.name: Shortest distance
patterns.howItWorks: The straight-line gap is the hypotenuse of the two net legs.

patterns.name: 3-4-5 shortcut
patterns.howItWorks: Recognise common Pythagorean triples to skip the square-root.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Final direction
workedTypes.idea: Track turns, name the facing. What makes it different: no distance needed.
workedTypes.problem: A man faces North, turns right, then right again. Which direction does he now face?

steps.action: North, turn right → East; turn right again → South.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Straight-line distance
workedTypes.idea: Net legs then Pythagoras. What makes it different: right-angle path.
workedTypes.problem: Walk 4 km East, then 3 km North. How far are you from the start (straight line)?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Net displacement (back-tracking)
workedTypes.idea: Cancel opposite moves. What makes it different: moves in the same axis.
workedTypes.problem: Walk 10 m North, then 4 m South. How far and in which direction from start?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — L-shaped path distance
workedTypes.idea: Sum each axis, then Pythagoras. What makes it different: multiple legs.
workedTypes.problem: Walk 3 km East, 2 km North, 3 km East, 2 km North. Straight-line distance from start?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Shadow / time clue
workedTypes.idea: Sun-based direction. What makes it different: infer facing from shadow.
workedTypes.problem: In the early morning, a man's shadow falls to his left. Which direction is he facing?

steps.action: Morning sun rises in the East, so shadows fall West.
Shadow on his left is West → he faces North.
steps.why: If left = West, he faces North.

traps: Not drawing the path — mental tracking of turns causes errors.
Confusing left/right turns; a right turn is clockwise.
Adding opposite moves instead of subtracting them (back-tracking).
Forgetting to use Pythagoras for the straight-line distance on an L-path.
Mixing up 'distance travelled' (sum of all legs) with 'straight-line distance' (hypotenuse).
understand.memoryHook: Draw axes (N up), plot each move, subtract opposite legs, and finish with √(EW² + NS²). Keep 3-4-5, 6-8-10, 5-12-13 in mind to skip the maths.

crack.oneLine: Draw the path on N-S/E-W axes, add moves per axis (opposite moves cancel), then the shortest distance is √(net-EW² + net-NS²) — spot 3-4-5 style triples to answer instantly.

techniques.name: Axis totals
techniques.method: Keep two running totals: East-West and North-South.
techniques.howToApply: Add E/W to one, N/S to the other.
techniques.timeSaved: No need to redraw every leg.

techniques.name: Triple spotting
techniques.method: Recognise Pythagorean triples for the hypotenuse.
techniques.whenFails: Legs that aren't a known triple.

techniques.name: Turn clock
techniques.method: Right = clockwise 90°, left = anticlockwise 90°.

techniques.name: Shadow rule
techniques.method: Sunrise East → morning shadows West; sunset West → evening shadows East.
techniques.howToApply: Use shadow side to fix facing.

drill.question: Two right turns from N — facing?

crack.whyItWorks: Perpendicular moves are independent, so tracking East-West and North-South separately is exact. The straight-line gap between start and end is the hypotenuse of these two net legs — pure Pythagoras.
bestFor: Any right-angled path of North/South/East/West moves.
Turn-only questions asking for final facing direction.
notFor: Paths with 45° or other odd angles need trigonometry, not simple triples.
'Distance travelled' asks for the sum of legs, not the hypotenuse — read carefully.
crack.practicePattern: Every direction question: draw axes, total each axis (cancel opposites), and finish with the hypotenuse or the final facing direction.

groups.description: Place people or objects in rows and around tables using the given clues.

topics.description: Seat people in a row using the given conditions.

videos.label: Puzzle & Seating Arrangement — Beginner to Pro
videos.note: Step-by-step method for linear & row-based puzzles

understand.realLife: Deciding who sits where at a dinner table — 'Ravi must sit next to Meena, but not at the ends' — is a seating puzzle. You juggle a handful of rules until only one arrangement fits. Exams love these because they test whether you can hold several conditions in mind at once and combine them cleanly.
understand.coreIdea: You get a group of people (or objects) to place in a single straight row, plus clues like 'A is to the left of B', 'C is at an end', 'exactly two people sit between D and E'. Your job is to build the one seating that satisfies every clue. The winning method is to DRAW the row as numbered blank seats and pencil in the most fixed clues first.
understand.howToThink: Draw the row as empty slots: 1 2 3 4 5 6 (left to right). Decide the facing direction — if everyone faces North, then 'left' means towards seat 1 and 'right' towards the last seat. Start with the strongest clues: fixed positions ('X is 3rd from the left') and end clues, then place 'immediate neighbour' clues, then looser 'somewhere to the left/right' clues. Use 'exactly n between' to fix gaps. When a clue can go two ways, note both possibilities and eliminate the one that clashes later.

patterns.name: Draw the row
patterns.howItWorks: Number the seats and note the facing direction before placing anyone.

patterns.name: Position from an end
patterns.whatItLooksLike: 'kth from left' = seat k; 'kth from right' = seat (n−k+1)
patterns.howItWorks: Convert 'from the right' to an actual seat number.

patterns.name: Between count
patterns.howItWorks: m people between means their seat numbers differ by m+1.

patterns.name: Fix strongest first
patterns.whatItLooksLike: ends & exact positions → neighbours → loose clues
patterns.howItWorks: Lock the most restrictive clues before flexible ones.
patterns.example: Place 'at the end' before 'somewhere left of'.

patterns.name: Two-case split
patterns.whatItLooksLike: ambiguous clue → try both, keep the consistent one
patterns.howItWorks: When a clue allows two placements, branch and discard the contradictory branch.
patterns.example: 'A left of B' with two gaps → test each.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Fixed position
workedTypes.idea: Place from an end. What makes it different: one exact seat given.
workedTypes.problem: Five people P,Q,R,S,T sit in a row. R is 2nd from the left. Which seat is R in?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Immediate neighbour
workedTypes.idea: Adjacent placement. What makes it different: 'next to' clue.
workedTypes.problem: In a row of 4 (seats 1–4), A is at seat 1 and B sits immediately to A's right. Which seat is B?

steps.action: Immediately right of seat 1 is seat 2.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Exactly n between
workedTypes.idea: Fix a gap. What makes it different: counting seats between.
workedTypes.problem: In a row of 6, exactly 2 people sit between M and N. If M is in seat 2, where is N (to M's right)?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Ends and middle
workedTypes.idea: Combine end clues. What makes it different: two ends fixed.
workedTypes.problem: Four friends W,X,Y,Z in seats 1–4. W is at the left end, Z at the right end, X is left of Y. Who is in seat 2?

steps.action: W=1, Z=4; X and Y take seats 2,3 with X left of Y.
steps.why: Ends fixed, order clue for the middle.
X must be the smaller seat.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two-case elimination
workedTypes.idea: Branch then discard. What makes it different: ambiguous clue.
workedTypes.problem: In a row of 3 (1–3), A is not at an end and B is left of A. Where is C?

steps.action: A not at an end → A=2; B left of A → B=1.

traps: Not drawing the row — mental placement leads to clashes.
Confusing left/right when the facing direction isn't fixed first.
Miscounting 'between': 2 between means seats differ by 3, not 2.
Placing loose clues before fixed ones, causing rework.
Forgetting to test both branches of an ambiguous clue.
understand.memoryHook: Draw numbered seats, fix facing direction, then lock ends & exact positions first, neighbours next, loose clues last. 'm between' → gap of m+1.

crack.oneLine: Draw the row as numbered seats, set the facing direction, place the most fixed clues (ends, exact positions) first, then neighbours, then loose clues — and read 'm between' as a seat-gap of m+1.

techniques.name: Fixed-first
techniques.method: Anchor exact/end positions before anything flexible.

techniques.name: Gap rule
techniques.method: 'm people between' → seat difference m+1.
techniques.whenFails: If direction between them is unknown (try both).

techniques.name: End conversion
techniques.method: kth from right = seat (n−k+1).

techniques.name: Two-case tree
techniques.method: Branch ambiguous clues, discard the clashing branch.
techniques.example: 'A left of B' unresolved → test both.
techniques.whenFails: When too many branches — then wait for a tighter clue.

crack.whyItWorks: A row is just ordered positions 1..n. Every clue is a constraint on those positions; placing the tightest constraints first shrinks the possibilities fastest, so the unique arrangement falls out with minimal backtracking.
bestFor: Single-row puzzles with position, neighbour and between clues.
Puzzles where at least one fixed/end clue anchors the row.
notFor: If facing direction is mixed (some face North, some South), redefine left/right per person.
Very loose puzzles with no fixed clue may have multiple solutions — check the question allows a unique answer.
crack.practicePattern: Every linear puzzle: draw numbered seats, fix direction, lock fixed clues, then neighbours, then loose clues, and read off the seat asked.

topics.description: Seat people around a table, facing in or out.

understand.realLife: Seating guests around a round dining table is trickier than a row: there is no 'first' or 'last' seat, and whether someone's left is your left depends on which way they face. Circular-seating puzzles capture exactly this — arranging people around a table using neighbour and gap clues.
understand.coreIdea: People sit around a circular (or rectangular) table, either all facing the centre or all facing outward (or a mix). You place them using clues like 'A is to the immediate left of B' or 'C sits third to the right of D'. The key twist: when people face the CENTRE, their left/right is the mirror of yours; when they face OUTWARD, it matches your view from outside.
understand.howToThink: Draw a circle with n equally spaced seats and fix the facing rule first. If everyone faces the centre, then a person's LEFT is clockwise and their RIGHT is anticlockwise (from your top-down view). If everyone faces outward, it reverses: left = anticlockwise, right = clockwise. 'kth to the right of X' means count k seats in X's right direction. Because the table is a loop, positions wrap around: seat after n comes back to seat 1. Fix one person as a reference point (say seat 1) to break the rotational symmetry, then place the rest relative to them.

patterns.name: Fix facing first
patterns.howItWorks: Decide the left/right convention before placing anyone.
patterns.example: Facing centre → your left goes clockwise.

patterns.name: Anchor one person
patterns.whatItLooksLike: put reference person at a fixed seat
patterns.howItWorks: Breaks the rotational symmetry so positions become definite.

patterns.name: kth to the right
patterns.whatItLooksLike: count k seats in the right direction, wrapping around
patterns.howItWorks: Move k seats the correct way; loop past the last seat back to the first.
patterns.example: 3rd right of X = X + 3 seats clockwise (if right=clockwise).

patterns.name: Immediate neighbours
patterns.howItWorks: 'Immediate left/right' = the seat next to them in that direction.

patterns.name: Opposite seat
patterns.whatItLooksLike: for even n, opposite = seat + n/2
patterns.howItWorks: Directly across the table is n/2 seats away.
patterns.example: 8 seats → opposite of seat 1 is seat 5.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Immediate neighbour (facing centre)
workedTypes.idea: Left = clockwise. What makes it different: mirror of a row.
workedTypes.problem: 5 people sit around a table facing the centre. B is to the immediate left of A. If left is clockwise, is B clockwise or anticlockwise from A?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — kth to the right
workedTypes.idea: Count around the loop. What makes it different: wrap-around.
workedTypes.problem: 6 people 1–6 clockwise, facing centre (right = anticlockwise). Who is 2nd to the right of the person at seat 1?

steps.action: Right = anticlockwise, so move from seat 1 anticlockwise: 6, then 5.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Opposite seat
workedTypes.idea: Across the table. What makes it different: uses n/2.
workedTypes.problem: 8 people sit around a table (seats 1–8). Who sits directly opposite seat 3?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Facing outward
workedTypes.idea: Reversed left/right. What makes it different: outward facing.
workedTypes.problem: People face OUTWARD around a table. For them, left is anticlockwise. Who is to the immediate left of the person at seat 1 (seats 1–5 clockwise)?

steps.action: Outward: left = anticlockwise, so from seat 1 go anticlockwise → seat 5.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Mixed clue solve
workedTypes.idea: Combine neighbour + gap. What makes it different: anchor then build.
workedTypes.problem: 4 people W,X,Y,Z around a table facing centre. W is opposite Y, and X is to W's immediate left (clockwise). Where is Z?

steps.action: Place W at seat 1; opposite (4 seats, n/2=2) → Y at seat 3.
X immediate left (clockwise) of W = seat 2; Z takes seat 4.

workedTypes.answer: Z is at seat 4 (immediate right of W).

traps: Forgetting to fix the facing rule — left/right flips between centre-facing and outward.
Not anchoring one person, leaving the arrangement rotationally ambiguous.
Counting 'kth to the right' the wrong way around the loop.
Ignoring wrap-around: after the last seat you return to the first.
Assuming a row-style 'ends' — a circle has no ends.
understand.memoryHook: Fix facing first (centre → left = clockwise; outward → left = anticlockwise), anchor one person, then count around the loop, wrapping past the last seat.

crack.oneLine: Set the facing rule (centre-facing: left = clockwise; outward: reversed), anchor one person to kill rotational symmetry, then count neighbours and 'kth to the right' around the loop, wrapping past the last seat.

techniques.name: Facing flip
techniques.method: Centre → left = clockwise; outward → left = anticlockwise.
techniques.whenFails: Mixed facing — handle each person separately.

techniques.name: Anchor
techniques.method: Fix one person at a reference seat.

techniques.name: Opposite rule
techniques.method: For even n, opposite = seat + n/2.
techniques.whenFails: Odd n has no exact opposite.

techniques.name: Loop count
techniques.method: Count k seats in the right direction, wrapping.

clueToMethod.method: Count k seats that way, wrap around.

drill.question: Facing centre: left is which way?
Facing outward: left is which way?

crack.whyItWorks: A circle is positions 0..n−1 modulo n. Fixing the facing rule turns left/right into a definite direction, and anchoring one person removes the only symmetry (rotation). Every clue then becomes an exact modular step, so the seating is determined.
bestFor: Round-table puzzles with clear facing and at least one anchor clue.
Even-n tables where 'opposite' clues apply.
notFor: Mixed-facing tables need per-person left/right handling.
Reflections can still be ambiguous if only relative clues are given — check for a fixing clue.
crack.practicePattern: Every circular puzzle: fix facing, anchor one person, then step around the loop for neighbours, kth-to-the-right and opposite clues.

groups.description: Combine many conditions to fill grids, floors, days and slots.

topics.description: Combine many conditions to fill grids, floors and boxes.

understand.realLife: Working out which flat each family lives on in a building, given hints like 'the Sharmas live above the Guptas but below the Reddys', is a classic puzzle. You keep combining clues until only one layout fits. Exams use these grid/floor/box puzzles to test how well you organise many conditions at once.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a set of items (people, floors, boxes, colours…) and several conditions, and you must work out the complete arrangement. The reliable method is a GRID (table): list items down one side and possible positions across the top, then tick/cross cells as clues force or forbid placements. Fill the most restrictive clues first, and use elimination — when only one option is left for a slot, it must be true.
understand.howToThink: Set up a table. For a floor puzzle, rows = people, columns = floor numbers (bottom = 1). Translate each clue into ticks (✓ = must be here) and crosses (✗ = cannot be here). Key relations: 'above/below' fixes a higher/lower number; 'immediately above' means +1 floor; 'exactly k floors apart' means the numbers differ by k. When a row or column has all-but-one crossed out, the survivor is forced. Branch on the biggest either/or clue and discard branches that hit a contradiction.

patterns.name: Build a grid
patterns.howItWorks: Rows = items, columns = slots; mark ✓/✗ per clue.

patterns.name: Above / below
patterns.howItWorks: Fix relative order on the numbered scale.

patterns.name: Immediately above
patterns.howItWorks: Directly above means exactly one level higher.
patterns.example: X right on top of Y.

patterns.name: k apart
patterns.howItWorks: 'Exactly k floors between-scale apart' → difference k.

patterns.name: Elimination
patterns.howItWorks: If a slot has all-but-one crossed, the last is true.
patterns.example: Only floor 4 free for C → C on 4.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Simple floor order
workedTypes.idea: Order on a scale. What makes it different: pure above/below.
workedTypes.problem: In a 3-floor building (1 bottom, 3 top), A is above B, and C is on the top. Who is on floor 1?

steps.action: C = floor 3; A above B leaves floors 1,2 with A higher → A=2, B=1.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Immediately above
workedTypes.idea: Adjacent levels. What makes it different: +1 clue.
workedTypes.problem: 4 floors. P is immediately above Q. Q is on floor 2. Which floor is P?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Grid elimination
workedTypes.idea: Cross out to force. What makes it different: two-attribute grid.
workedTypes.problem: Three people (A,B,C) each like one colour (red, blue, green). A doesn't like red or blue. B likes blue. What does C like?

steps.action: A likes green (not red/blue); B likes blue.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — k apart
workedTypes.idea: Gap on a scale. What makes it different: distance clue.
workedTypes.problem: 5 floors. R is on floor 1. S is exactly 3 floors apart from R (above). Which floor is S?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two-case branch
workedTypes.idea: Try both, discard clash. What makes it different: ambiguous clue.
workedTypes.problem: 3 floors. X is not on floor 2. Y is above X. Where is X?

steps.action: X ≠ 2, and Y above X, so X can't be top → X = floor 1.
steps.why: X=3 leaves no room for Y above.

traps: Not drawing a grid — juggling clues mentally causes errors.
Ignoring the most restrictive clue and starting with vague ones.
Confusing 'above' (some level higher) with 'immediately above' (+1).
Forgetting elimination: the last remaining option in a slot is forced.
Not branching cleanly on either/or clues, so contradictions slip through.
understand.memoryHook: Grid it: items × slots, mark ✓/✗. Fix the tightest clues first, then eliminate — the last option standing in any row or column is the answer.

crack.oneLine: Draw an items × positions grid, translate each clue into ✓/✗, place the most restrictive clues first, and use elimination — when a row or column has one option left, it is forced.

techniques.name: Grid method
techniques.method: Tabulate items vs positions, mark ✓/✗.

techniques.name: Restrictive-first
techniques.method: Start with clues fixing exact positions.

techniques.name: Last-one-standing
techniques.method: If a slot has all-but-one crossed, fill the survivor.

techniques.name: Branch & kill
techniques.method: Split ambiguous clues, discard the branch that contradicts.

drill.question: One colour left after red, blue used?

crack.whyItWorks: Every puzzle is a constraint-satisfaction problem over a finite grid. Marking ✓/✗ turns clues into concrete eliminations, and once a row or column is reduced to one option, logic forces it — no guessing needed if you start from the tightest clues.
bestFor: Floor, box, colour and category puzzles with definite clues.
Puzzles that have a unique solution (most exam ones).
notFor: Under-constrained puzzles may have several solutions — check the question expects a unique one.
Very large puzzles still need the grid; do not shortcut by memory.
crack.practicePattern: Every puzzle: build the grid, mark ✓/✗ from clues (tightest first), eliminate to force cells, and read off the asked position.

topics.description: Match days, slots or people using constraint clues.

understand.realLife: Planning who works which shift, or which subject is taught on which day, is a scheduling puzzle. You match one set (people/tasks) to another (days/slots) so every clue is satisfied. It is the same skill a manager uses to build a rota without clashes.
understand.coreIdea: You are given two (or more) sets — e.g. people and days, or tasks and time-slots — and clues linking them ('Meeting is not on Monday', 'Anil's task is the day after Bina's'). You must produce the unique matching. Use a MATCHING GRID: one set as rows, the other as columns, and tick/cross to build a one-to-one assignment, obeying order clues ('before/after') on the day scale.
understand.howToThink: Lay out days/slots in order (Mon=1 … or slot 1,2,3…). Make a grid of people/tasks × days. Each person takes exactly one day and each day one person (one-to-one), so a ✓ in a cell crosses out the rest of that row AND that column. Order clues use the day numbers: 'A before B' → day(A) < day(B); 'A the day after B' → day(A) = day(B)+1; 'A and B on consecutive days' → |day(A)−day(B)| = 1. Fix negatives ('not on Wednesday') as crosses, place order clues, then eliminate.

patterns.name: Matching grid
patterns.howItWorks: A ✓ crosses out the rest of its row and column.

patterns.name: Before / after
patterns.howItWorks: Fix order on the day scale.

patterns.name: Day after
patterns.howItWorks: Immediately following day.

patterns.name: Consecutive
patterns.howItWorks: Adjacent days, order unknown.

patterns.name: One-to-one cross-out
patterns.howItWorks: Each slot and each item used once.
patterns.example: A on Mon → nobody else on Mon.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Simple negative
workedTypes.idea: Cross out a forbidden slot. What makes it different: 'not on' clue.
workedTypes.problem: Three tasks on Mon, Tue, Wed. Task P is not on Monday and not on Tuesday. Which day is P?

steps.action: P ≠ Mon, P ≠ Tue → only Wed left.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Day after
workedTypes.idea: Immediately following. What makes it different: +1 day.
workedTypes.problem: A's meeting is the day after B's. If B is on Tuesday, when is A?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Before/after order
workedTypes.idea: Fix order on the scale. What makes it different: relative order.
workedTypes.problem: Three events X,Y,Z on three consecutive days. X is before Y, Y is before Z. Which event is first?

steps.action: X < Y < Z on the day scale.
X is on the first day.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — One-to-one matching
workedTypes.idea: Cross row & column. What makes it different: unique assignment.
workedTypes.problem: Anil, Bina, Charu take Mon, Tue, Wed (one each). Anil is on Tuesday. Bina is not on Monday. Which day is Charu?

steps.action: Anil=Tue; Bina ≠ Mon and ≠ Tue → Bina=Wed.
Charu takes the last day = Monday.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Consecutive + fixed
workedTypes.idea: Combine gap and anchor. What makes it different: multiple clues.
workedTypes.problem: Four talks Mon–Thu. Talk A is on Monday. B and C are on consecutive days, both after A, and D is on Thursday. Which days are B and C?

steps.action: A=Mon, D=Thu; B and C fill Tue,Wed consecutively.
B and C are on Tuesday and Wednesday.

traps: Not treating the assignment as one-to-one — a ✓ must clear its whole row and column.
Reading 'day after' as 'some day later' instead of exactly +1.
Ignoring the ordering of days when applying before/after clues.
Missing that 'consecutive' leaves the order (which is earlier) open.
Not crossing out forbidden slots early ('not on Monday').
understand.memoryHook: Order the days, build a person × day grid, cross out forbidden cells, place +1 and before/after clues on the day scale, and remember: one tick clears its row AND column.

crack.oneLine: Lay days in order, build a one-to-one matching grid, cross out 'not on' cells, apply before/after and day-after (+1) clues on the day scale, and clear each ✓'s whole row and column.

techniques.name: One-to-one clear
techniques.method: A ✓ crosses the rest of its row and column.

techniques.name: Day-scale order
techniques.method: Number days; before = smaller, after = larger.

techniques.name: Plus-one
techniques.method: 'Day after' = +1 exactly.

techniques.name: Negatives-first
techniques.method: Cross out all 'not on' cells before positive clues.

clueToMethod.method: A ✓ clears its row and column.

drill.question: P not Mon, not Tue (of Mon–Wed) → ?

crack.whyItWorks: A schedule is a one-to-one mapping between items and ordered slots. The grid enforces uniqueness (row/column clearing) and the day numbers make order clues exact, so combining eliminations pins down the only valid rota.
bestFor: People-to-days, tasks-to-slots and similar one-to-one matchings.
Schedules with clear negative and order clues.
notFor: If several people can share a day, it is not one-to-one — adjust the grid rules.
Under-constrained schedules may allow multiple valid rotas.
crack.practicePattern: Every scheduling puzzle: order the slots, grid the two sets, cross out negatives, apply +1/before/after clues, and clear rows/columns until the matching is unique.

groups.description: Decide what truly follows from given statements — conclusions, assumptions and actions.

topics.description: Decide which conclusions truly follow from the statements.

videos.label: Complete Syllogism — concepts & tricks (Adda247)
videos.note: 830K views — Venn method from basics to Mains-level

understand.realLife: If someone tells you 'all doctors are graduates' and 'all graduates can read', you instantly know 'all doctors can read'. Syllogisms just formalise this everyday chaining of statements — you decide which conclusions MUST be true given the premises, using nothing outside what is stated.
understand.coreIdea: You are given two (or more) statements about groups — using 'All', 'Some', 'No', 'Some…not' — and one or more conclusions. You must say which conclusions definitely follow. The safest method is to DRAW VENN DIAGRAMS (overlapping circles) for the statements, then check whether each conclusion is true in EVERY possible diagram. If a conclusion can fail in even one valid diagram, it does not follow.
understand.howToThink: Four statement types: 'All A are B' (circle A inside circle B), 'No A is B' (separate circles), 'Some A are B' (circles overlap), 'Some A are not B' (part of A outside B). Rules of thumb: (1) two 'All' statements chain: All A→B, All B→C gives All A→C. (2) 'No' is reversible: No A is B = No B is A. (3) 'Some A are B' is reversible: Some A are B = Some B are A. (4) A conclusion follows only if it holds in every diagram you can legally draw. Watch for 'possibility' conclusions and complementary pairs ('Some A are B' vs 'No A is B') — at least one of a complementary pair is always true (the 'either-or' case).

patterns.name: All A are B
patterns.howItWorks: Every A is also a B.

patterns.name: No A is B
patterns.howItWorks: No overlap; reversible.

patterns.name: Some A are B
patterns.howItWorks: At least one common; reversible.

patterns.name: Chaining All
patterns.whatItLooksLike: All A→B, All B→C ⇒ All A→C
patterns.howItWorks: Two universal statements combine.
patterns.example: All A are B, all B are C → all A are C.

patterns.name: Either-or
patterns.howItWorks: 'Some A are B' or 'No A is B' — if neither definitely follows alone but together cover all cases, the answer is 'either follows'.
patterns.example: Either 'Some A are B' or 'No A is B'.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Chaining All
workedTypes.idea: Two universals combine. What makes it different: straightforward chain.
workedTypes.problem: Statements: All pens are pencils. All pencils are erasers. Conclusion: All pens are erasers. Does it follow?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Some (reversible)
workedTypes.idea: Overlap gives 'some'. What makes it different: existential.
workedTypes.problem: Statements: All cats are animals. Some animals are pets. Conclusion: Some cats are pets. Does it follow?

steps.action: The 'some animals that are pets' need not include any cat.
steps.why: Overlap may avoid the cat region.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — No (reversible)
workedTypes.idea: Separation. What makes it different: negative statement.
workedTypes.problem: Statements: No dog is a cat. Conclusion: No cat is a dog. Does it follow?

steps.action: 'No A is B' means the circles are separate.
So 'No cat is a dog' follows.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Either-or (complementary)
workedTypes.idea: Two conclusions cover all cases. What makes it different: possibility split.
workedTypes.problem: Statements: Some A are B. All B are C. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. No A is C. Which follows?

steps.action: Some A are B and all B are C → those A are C, so I follows.
steps.why: The overlapping A must be in C.
There is definitely some A in C.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Neither follows
workedTypes.idea: Both fail in some diagram. What makes it different: trap conclusions.
workedTypes.problem: Statements: Some books are pens. Some pens are red. Conclusions: I. Some books are red. II. All pens are books. Which follows?

steps.action: The red pens need not be the book-pens; and pens aren't all books.
steps.why: Both can fail in a valid diagram.

traps: Using real-world knowledge instead of only the given statements.
Treating 'Some A are B' as if it guarantees 'Some A are not B' (it does not).
Assuming two 'Some' statements chain — they never give a definite conclusion.
Forgetting a conclusion must hold in EVERY possible diagram, not just one.
Missing the 'either-or' case for complementary conclusion pairs.
understand.memoryHook: Draw Venn circles for the statements, then test each conclusion in every legal diagram. Only 'All+All' chains and reversible 'No'/'Some' are safe; two 'Some' never chain.

crack.oneLine: Draw Venn diagrams for the premises and accept a conclusion only if it is true in EVERY possible diagram — 'All' chains, 'No' and 'Some' reverse, but two 'Some' statements never give a definite conclusion.

techniques.name: Venn test
techniques.method: Draw all legal diagrams; keep only conclusions true in all.

techniques.name: All-chains
techniques.method: All A→B and All B→C give All A→C.

techniques.name: Reversibility
techniques.method: 'No A is B' = 'No B is A'; 'Some A are B' = 'Some B are A'.
techniques.example: No dog cat → no cat dog.

techniques.name: Either-or spot
techniques.method: If two conclusions are complementary and neither alone is sure, answer 'either follows'.
techniques.example: Some A are C / No A is C.

clueToMethod.method: Reverse it; but two 'Some' don't chain.
Ignore world knowledge; test the diagram.

drill.question: Conclusion must hold in how many diagrams?

crack.whyItWorks: A syllogism is valid only if the conclusion is forced in every model of the premises. Venn diagrams enumerate those models visually, so a conclusion that survives all diagrams is logically guaranteed — exactly the definition of 'follows'.
bestFor: Standard All/No/Some/Some-not statements.
Either-or and possibility questions once you draw all cases.
notFor: Do not import outside facts — only the given statements count.
Long chains still need careful diagrams; do not eyeball them.
crack.practicePattern: Every syllogism: draw Venn diagrams for the premises, then accept only conclusions true in every legal diagram; check complementary pairs for 'either-or'.

topics.description: Judge whether conclusions logically follow given statements.

videos.label: Statement & Conclusion — Tricks & Shortcuts (CareerRide)
videos.note: Placement-focused: treat statements as true, avoid assumptions

videos.label: Statement & Conclusions — SSC reasoning (Shobhit Sir)
videos.note: More worked exam questions for practice

understand.realLife: When you read a notice — 'The library will stay open till 10 pm during exams' — you can safely conclude the library recognises students need extra study time, but you cannot conclude it will be open at midnight. Statement-conclusion questions test exactly this: what can be logically drawn from a passage, and what over-reaches.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a short statement (a fact, notice or argument) and one or more conclusions. Assuming the statement is TRUE, you decide which conclusions definitely follow. The golden rule: a conclusion follows only if it is directly supported by the statement — no outside knowledge, no big assumptions, no 'extreme' words unless stated.
understand.howToThink: Treat the statement as 100% true. Test each conclusion against three filters: (1) Directly stated or a necessary implication? → follows. (2) Needs extra information or an assumption not given? → does not follow. (3) Too strong/extreme ('all', 'never', 'only', 'best') when the statement is moderate? → usually does not follow. For 'either-or' options, a pair of opposite conclusions where the statement supports one side gives a single answer, not 'either'. Restatements of the statement in different words DO follow.

patterns.name: Assume true
patterns.howItWorks: Never question the statement; only judge the conclusions.
patterns.example: If it says sales rose, accept that.

patterns.name: Direct support
patterns.howItWorks: Only conclusions the statement forces are valid.

patterns.name: No extra assumption
patterns.whatItLooksLike: needs outside info ⇒ does not follow
patterns.howItWorks: Reject conclusions requiring unstated facts.
patterns.example: 'Open till 10' → 'has many books' does not follow.

patterns.name: Beware extremes
patterns.howItWorks: Strong words rarely follow from moderate statements.

patterns.name: Restatement
patterns.howItWorks: Saying the same thing differently is valid.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Direct implication
workedTypes.idea: Straight from text. What makes it different: obvious support.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'All students must wear ID cards on campus.' Conclusion: Students on campus are expected to carry ID cards. Does it follow?

steps.action: The statement directly requires ID cards on campus.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Extra assumption (reject)
workedTypes.idea: Needs outside info. What makes it different: unsupported.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'The company launched a new phone.' Conclusion: The new phone will be a best-seller. Does it follow?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Extreme wording (reject)
