workedTypes.idea: Too strong. What makes it different: 'all/never'.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Some employees attended the workshop.' Conclusion: All employees are interested in training. Does it follow?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Notice/advice implication
workedTypes.idea: Purpose behind a notice. What makes it different: intent.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Keep the medicine out of reach of children.' Conclusion: The medicine could be harmful to children. Does it follow?

steps.action: The warning implies a risk to children.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two conclusions (only one)
workedTypes.idea: Judge each separately. What makes it different: I and II.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Prices of vegetables have risen sharply this month.' I. Vegetables are costlier now than last month. II. Prices will fall next month. Which follows?

steps.action: I restates the rise; II predicts the future (unsupported).
steps.why: Only I is grounded in the text.

traps: Bringing in real-world knowledge instead of using only the statement.
Accepting extreme conclusions ('all', 'never', 'best') from moderate statements.
Rejecting a valid restatement because it uses different words.
Predicting the future when the statement only describes the present.
Choosing 'either-or' when the statement clearly supports just one side.
understand.memoryHook: Assume the statement is true, then accept a conclusion only if it is directly stated or necessarily implied — reject anything needing outside facts or extreme words.

crack.oneLine: Take the statement as fully true, then accept a conclusion only if it is directly stated or a necessary implication — reject predictions, outside knowledge and extreme 'all/never/only' wording.

techniques.name: Direct-support check
techniques.method: Ask: is this stated or forced by the statement?

techniques.name: Assumption filter
techniques.method: If it needs unstated info, reject.

techniques.name: Extreme-word alarm
techniques.method: 'All/never/only/best' beyond text → reject.
techniques.whenFails: When the statement itself is extreme.

techniques.name: Restatement accept
techniques.method: Paraphrases of the statement follow.

crack.whyItWorks: A conclusion 'follows' only if it is entailed by the statement alone. Filtering for direct support, no-extra-assumption and no-overreach mirrors logical entailment, so surviving conclusions are exactly the valid ones.
bestFor: Fact, notice and simple-argument statements.
Two-conclusion items judged independently.
notFor: Do not use general knowledge, only the statement.
Genuinely ambiguous statements may need the exam's own convention — default to strict entailment.
crack.practicePattern: Every item: assume the statement true, test each conclusion for direct support, reject assumptions/predictions/extremes, accept faithful restatements.

topics.description: Find the hidden assumption behind a statement.

understand.realLife: When a shop puts up a sign 'Buy now, pay later', it is silently assuming customers trust it enough to pay later. An assumption is something taken for granted — not said, but necessary for the statement to make sense. Spotting these hidden beliefs is a key reasoning skill.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a statement (often an advertisement, appeal or plan) and some assumptions. An assumption is something the speaker MUST be taking for granted for the statement to hold. You decide which assumptions are implicit. The test: if the assumption were false, would the statement fall apart? If yes, it is a valid assumption.
understand.howToThink: Use the NEGATION TEST: negate the assumption; if the statement no longer makes sense or its purpose fails, the assumption is implicit (valid). Rules: (1) an assumption must be UNSTATED — if it's written in the statement, it is not an assumption. (2) It must be NECESSARY, not merely possible. (3) Avoid over-broad or extreme assumptions. (4) An assumption is usually positive/supportive of the statement's aim; if the statement's goal needs it, it's implicit.

patterns.name: Assumption = taken for granted
patterns.howItWorks: Not written, yet required for the statement to work.

patterns.name: Negation test
patterns.howItWorks: If denying it breaks the statement, it is assumed.
patterns.example: If no one trusts the shop, 'pay later' fails.

patterns.name: Must be unstated
patterns.whatItLooksLike: written in statement ⇒ not an assumption
patterns.howItWorks: Explicit facts are not assumptions.
patterns.example: Stated price is not an assumption.

patterns.name: Necessary, not possible
patterns.howItWorks: Only necessary beliefs count.
patterns.example: 'Ad will be seen' is necessary for advertising.

patterns.name: Avoid extremes
patterns.howItWorks: Sweeping assumptions rarely hold.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Advertisement
workedTypes.idea: Why advertise? What makes it different: implicit reach.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Advertise your product in our newspaper for wide reach.' Assumption: People read the newspaper. Is it implicit?

steps.action: Negate: if no one reads it, the ad gives no reach.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Appeal / notice
workedTypes.idea: What must be true. What makes it different: audience can act.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Please switch off lights when leaving the room.' Assumption: People are able to switch off lights. Is it implicit?

steps.action: Negate: if no one can switch them off, the appeal is pointless.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Stated fact (not an assumption)
workedTypes.idea: Written ⇒ not assumed. What makes it different: explicit.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'This medicine cures fever within 3 days.' Assumption: The medicine cures fever. Is it implicit?

steps.action: The cure is explicitly stated, not assumed.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Extreme (reject)
workedTypes.idea: Too broad. What makes it different: over-generalised.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'Our coaching improved many students' scores.' Assumption: All students who join will top the exam. Is it implicit?

steps.action: 'Many improved' does not require 'all will top'.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Plan / policy
workedTypes.idea: Feasibility belief. What makes it different: plan can work.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'The city will run extra buses to reduce traffic.' Assumption: Extra buses can help reduce traffic. Is it implicit?

steps.action: Negate: if buses can't reduce traffic, the plan is pointless.

traps: Treating explicitly stated facts as assumptions.
Accepting over-broad or extreme assumptions.
Skipping the negation test and guessing.
Confusing an assumption (taken for granted) with a conclusion (drawn afterwards).
Assuming something merely possible rather than necessary.
understand.memoryHook: Negate the assumption: if the statement then collapses, it is implicit. It must be unstated, necessary and not extreme.

crack.oneLine: Apply the negation test — deny the assumption and if the statement's purpose collapses, it is implicit; the assumption must be unstated, necessary and not extreme.

techniques.name: Negation test
techniques.method: Negate the assumption; check if the statement fails.

techniques.name: Unstated check
techniques.method: If it's written in the statement, it's not an assumption.

techniques.name: Extreme filter
techniques.method: Reject sweeping 'all/every/always' assumptions.

techniques.name: Purpose link
techniques.method: Ask what belief the statement's goal needs.

clueToMethod.method: Assumes the audience can act on it.
Assumes the plan can achieve its aim.

drill.question: Can a stated fact be an assumption?
Assumption vs conclusion?
drill.answer: Taken for granted vs drawn after

crack.whyItWorks: An implicit assumption is a premise the statement silently depends on. Negating it and watching the statement fail is precisely how we detect a necessary premise, so the negation test reliably identifies valid assumptions.
bestFor: Advertisements, appeals, notices, plans and policies.
Any statement with a clear purpose to test against.
notFor: Statements with no clear aim make the negation test weak.
Do not accept assumptions that are merely possible, only necessary ones.
crack.practicePattern: Every item: run the negation test, confirm the assumption is unstated and necessary, and reject extremes.

topics.description: Judge cause-and-effect links and the right course of action from a statement.

understand.realLife: If heavy rain floods a city and the government then declares a holiday, the rain is the CAUSE, the flood the EFFECT, and the holiday a course of ACTION. Sorting events into cause, effect and sensible action is how we reason about news and decisions every day.
understand.coreIdea: Two related sub-topics. (a) Cause & Effect: given two events, decide which is the cause and which the effect, or whether they are independent/effects of a common cause. (b) Course of Action: given a problem statement, judge whether a suggested action is a proper, practical step that should follow. In both, you reason only from the statement, choosing the logically sound relationship or action.
understand.howToThink: Cause-effect options usually are: (1) A is cause, B is effect; (2) B is cause, A is effect; (3) both are independent causes; (4) both are effects of a common cause; (5) unrelated. Rule: the cause comes first in time and directly produces the effect. For Course of Action: a suggested action is valid if it (i) directly addresses the problem, (ii) is practical/feasible, and (iii) is not extreme or harmful. Reject actions that are impractical, over-reactive, or don't tackle the stated problem.

patterns.name: Cause before effect
patterns.howItWorks: The cause happens first and produces the effect.

patterns.name: Common cause
patterns.howItWorks: Two events may both be effects of a third cause.
patterns.example: Fever and cough from one infection.

patterns.name: Independent events
patterns.howItWorks: Sometimes two events are unrelated.
patterns.example: Exam date and a cricket match.

patterns.name: Valid action — addresses problem
patterns.howItWorks: Must tackle the actual problem.

patterns.name: Valid action — practical
patterns.howItWorks: Reject impractical or harsh actions.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Clear cause & effect
workedTypes.idea: Order in time. What makes it different: one causes the other.
workedTypes.problem: Event A: It rained heavily last night. Event B: The roads are waterlogged this morning. Relationship?

steps.action: Rain came first and produced the waterlogging.
A is the cause, B is the effect.

workedTypes.answer: A is cause, B is effect.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Common cause
workedTypes.idea: Both from one source. What makes it different: shared root.
workedTypes.problem: Event A: Many students were absent. Event B: Many teachers were absent. Both on the same day. Likely relationship?

steps.action: Neither directly causes the other; a common reason (e.g. a strike) fits.
Both are effects of a common cause.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Valid course of action
workedTypes.idea: Addresses the problem. What makes it different: practical fix.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'There is a severe water shortage in the city.' Action: The authorities should regulate water supply and promote saving. Should it follow?

steps.action: The action directly tackles the shortage and is practical.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Extreme action (reject)
workedTypes.idea: Impractical/harsh. What makes it different: over-reaction.
workedTypes.problem: Statement: 'A few students failed the test.' Action: Expel all failing students from the college. Should it follow?

steps.action: Expulsion is extreme and doesn't help the students improve.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Independent events
workedTypes.idea: No link. What makes it different: unrelated.
workedTypes.problem: Event A: The stock market rose today. Event B: A new film released today. Relationship?

steps.action: No causal connection between the two.

traps: Assuming that because B follows A in time, A must have caused B (correlation ≠ causation).
Missing the 'common cause' option when two events share a root.
Accepting extreme or impractical courses of action.
Choosing actions that don't address the actual problem.
Bringing in outside facts instead of reasoning from the statement.
understand.memoryHook: For cause-effect, ask 'which came first and produced the other?' — and consider a common cause. For actions, keep only steps that directly, practically and reasonably solve the problem.

crack.oneLine: Cause comes before effect (but watch for a common cause or coincidence); a course of action is valid only if it directly, practically and reasonably addresses the stated problem — reject extremes.

techniques.name: Time order
techniques.method: The earlier event that produces the other is the cause.

techniques.name: Common-cause check
techniques.method: If neither causes the other, ask if one root explains both.

techniques.name: Addresses-problem
techniques.method: Keep actions that tackle the stated issue.

techniques.name: Extreme reject
techniques.method: Discard harsh, impractical or over-broad actions.
techniques.whenFails: When the problem truly warrants strong action.

clueToMethod.clue: Two events, same time, shared root

drill.question: Cause comes when vs the effect?

crack.whyItWorks: Causation requires a real producing link, not just sequence; considering common-cause and independence avoids the post-hoc fallacy. Judging actions by relevance, feasibility and proportion mirrors sound decision-making, so only sensible steps survive.
bestFor: Event pairs with a plausible causal or common-cause link.
Problem statements with concrete, practical action options.
notFor: Coincidental events should be called independent, not causal.
Do not judge actions using outside facts — only the stated problem.
crack.practicePattern: Every item: for cause-effect, order the events and check common-cause/independence; for actions, keep only relevant, practical, proportionate steps.

groups.description: Order items from clues, model groups with Venn diagrams, and judge if the data decides the answer.

topics.description: Arrange people or objects by rank from partial clues.

understand.realLife: When a teacher says 'Ravi is 5th from the top and 8th from the bottom', you can work out exactly how many students are in the class. Ranking questions are just this: given a person's position from one or both ends, find their rank, the total, or someone else's position.
understand.coreIdea: You are told positions from the top/bottom (or left/right) and asked for the total number, a person's exact rank, or how two ranks relate. The reliable method is to draw a line of positions and use two tiny formulas built from the idea that a person's two ranks overlap on themselves (so you subtract 1 once).
understand.howToThink: Two core results. (1) Total = (rank from top) + (rank from bottom) − 1, because the person is counted from both ends and shared once. (2) People between two persons = |difference of their positions from the same end| − 1. Also, if a rank from the top is R in a group of N, the rank from the bottom is N − R + 1. Always count from the SAME end when comparing, and convert ends using these formulas.

patterns.name: Total from both ranks
patterns.howItWorks: Add the two ranks and subtract the double-counted person.

patterns.name: Convert ends
patterns.howItWorks: Flip a top-rank into a bottom-rank in a group of N.

patterns.name: People between
patterns.howItWorks: Positions from the same end, minus the shared gap.

patterns.name: Same-end rule
patterns.whatItLooksLike: compare ranks from one end only
patterns.howItWorks: Convert everything to 'from the top' before comparing.

patterns.name: Rank shift
patterns.whatItLooksLike: new rank = old rank ± positions moved
patterns.howItWorks: Moving up lowers the rank number; moving down raises it.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Total from both ends
workedTypes.idea: Add the two ranks. What makes it different: −1 for the shared person.
workedTypes.problem: In a row, Sita is 6th from the left and 4th from the right. How many people are in the row?

steps.why: Sita is counted from both ends once each.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Convert ends
workedTypes.idea: Flip top to bottom. What makes it different: known total.
workedTypes.problem: In a class of 30, Amit is 12th from the top. What is his rank from the bottom?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — People between
workedTypes.idea: Difference minus one. What makes it different: same-end ranks.
workedTypes.problem: In a queue, A is 4th and B is 10th from the front. How many people stand between them?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Rank after a shift
workedTypes.idea: Adjust the rank. What makes it different: movement.
workedTypes.problem: In a race, Ravi was 8th. He overtook 3 runners. What is his new position?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two people, find total
workedTypes.idea: Combine ranks and gap. What makes it different: mixed ends.
workedTypes.problem: In a row, P is 5th from left, Q is 6th from right, and exactly 3 people sit between them. How many are in the row?

steps.action: Total = pos(P from left) + between + pos(Q from right) = 5 + 3 + 6.
steps.why: P, the gap, and Q from the far end tile the whole row.
Add the three parts (no overlap here since P and Q are counted from opposite ends).

traps: Forgetting the −1 in Total = top + bottom − 1.
Comparing ranks measured from different ends without converting.
Counting the two people themselves as 'between' them.
Getting the direction of a shift wrong (up lowers the number).
Mixing 'position' with 'number of people' in gap problems.
understand.memoryHook: Total = top + bottom − 1. Between = |difference| − 1. Convert every rank to the same end before comparing.

crack.oneLine: Total = (rank from top) + (rank from bottom) − 1; people between two same-end ranks = |difference| − 1; and bottom rank = N − top + 1 — convert everything to one end first.

techniques.name: Both-ends total
techniques.method: Add the two ranks, subtract 1.
techniques.whenWorks: Both ranks of one person given.

techniques.name: End flip
techniques.method: bottom = N − top + 1.
techniques.howToApply: Plug N and the known rank.

techniques.name: Gap rule
techniques.method: between = |a−b| − 1 (same end).

techniques.name: Shift math
techniques.method: up → subtract, down → add the moves.

clueToMethod.clue: both 'from top' and 'from bottom' of one person
'rank from the other end' with total known

crack.whyItWorks: A line of N positions is counted once from each end; the person shared by both counts is added twice, so subtracting 1 gives the true total. Gaps drop the two endpoints, hence the −1 in the between-formula.
bestFor: Single-line rankings from top/bottom or left/right.
Race and queue position problems.
notFor: Two-dimensional (rows AND columns) rankings need the formula applied per direction.
Ties or equal ranks break the simple counting.
crack.practicePattern: Every ranking question: convert to one end, then apply Total = top+bottom−1 or between = |difference|−1.

topics.description: Model relationships between groups using Venn diagrams.

videos.label: Venn Diagram reasoning — master any question
videos.note: How to map group relationships & pick the right diagram

understand.realLife: Think of a class where some students play cricket, some play football, and a few play both. Drawing two overlapping circles instantly shows how many play only cricket, only football, or both. Venn diagrams turn confusing 'how many' word problems into a simple picture.
understand.coreIdea: You model groups as overlapping circles and either (a) pick the diagram that best fits a relationship (e.g. 'dogs, animals, cats') or (b) count how many items fall in each region given totals and overlaps. The trick is to fill the OVERLAP first, then work outward so nothing is double-counted.
understand.howToThink: For two sets: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| − |A ∩ B|. Fill the intersection first, then 'only A' = |A| − |A∩B| and 'only B' = |B| − |A∩B|. For three sets: |A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C| − |A∩B| − |B∩C| − |C∩A| + |A∩B∩C|. Start from the centre (all three) and subtract outward. For 'best diagram' questions, decide each pair's relationship: fully inside (all X are Y), separate (no overlap), or partial overlap (some).

patterns.name: Two-set union
patterns.howItWorks: Add both, subtract the shared part counted twice.

patterns.name: Only-A
patterns.howItWorks: Remove the overlap from A.

patterns.name: Three-set union
patterns.howItWorks: Inclusion–exclusion for three circles.

patterns.name: Fill centre first
patterns.howItWorks: Subtract inner regions from each overlap to avoid double counting.

patterns.name: Relationship pick
patterns.howItWorks: Classify each pair to choose the right diagram.
patterns.example: All pens are stationery → pen circle inside stationery.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Best diagram (nested)
workedTypes.idea: Classify relationships. What makes it different: no numbers.
workedTypes.problem: Which diagram fits: Dog, Animal, Cat?

steps.action: Dogs and cats are both animals but separate from each other.
steps.why: All dogs/cats are animals; no dog is a cat.

steps.action: Two separate circles (dog, cat) inside one big circle (animal).

workedTypes.answer: Dog and Cat circles, both inside Animal.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Two-set count
workedTypes.idea: Union formula. What makes it different: overlap given.
workedTypes.problem: In a group, 20 like tea, 15 like coffee, 5 like both. How many like at least one?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Only-one region
workedTypes.idea: Strip the overlap. What makes it different: 'only' asked.
workedTypes.problem: 40 study Maths, 25 study Physics, 10 study both. How many study only Maths?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Neither
workedTypes.idea: Total minus union. What makes it different: outside all circles.
workedTypes.problem: In a class of 50, 30 play cricket, 20 play football, 10 play both. How many play neither?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Three-set count
workedTypes.idea: Inclusion–exclusion. What makes it different: three circles.
workedTypes.problem: 60 read A, 50 read B, 40 read C; A∩B=20, B∩C=15, C∩A=10, all three=5. How many read at least one?

steps.why: Add singles, subtract pairs, add the triple.

traps: Forgetting to subtract the overlap (double counting the 'both').
In three sets, not adding back the triple overlap.
Confusing 'only A' with '|A|' (the whole circle).
Ignoring the 'neither' region outside all circles.
Picking a nested diagram when the groups only partly overlap.
understand.memoryHook: Two sets: A + B − both. Three sets: singles − pairs + triple. Always fill the centre first and work outward.

crack.oneLine: Fill the overlap (centre) first, then work outward: two-set union = A + B − both, three-set = singles − pairs + triple, and 'neither' = total − union.

techniques.name: Union subtract
techniques.method: Add sets, subtract each pairwise overlap once.

techniques.name: Centre-out
techniques.method: Start at the triple overlap, subtract inner regions from each pair.

techniques.name: Neither
techniques.method: total − union = outside all.

techniques.name: Relationship pick
techniques.method: Classify each pair: inside / separate / overlap.

drill.question: Three sets: add triple or subtract?

crack.whyItWorks: Inclusion–exclusion counts each element exactly once: overlaps added twice by the plain sum are removed, and the triple region removed too many times is added back. Filling the centre first makes every region a clean subtraction.
bestFor: Two- and three-category counting problems.
Best-fit diagram (relationship) questions.
notFor: Four or more sets are hard to draw — use the algebraic formula.
If overlaps aren't given, some counts are undetermined.
crack.practicePattern: Every Venn question: draw the circles, fill the overlaps first, then use A+B−both (or the three-set formula) and total−union for 'neither'.

topics.description: Apply given rules to pick the correct course of action.

understand.realLife: A company's HR rules say 'a candidate must be a graduate AND at least 21 to be shortlisted; if they fail age but are highly experienced, refer to the manager'. Given a candidate's details, you apply the rules to decide the outcome. Decision-making questions are exactly this: match facts against a set of conditions and pick the right action.
understand.coreIdea: You are given a set of eligibility rules (with main criteria and exceptions) and a candidate's data. You choose the correct decision: selected, rejected, refer to X, data insufficient, etc. The method is to check each condition one by one, apply exceptions only when triggered, and never add your own assumptions.
understand.howToThink: Treat each rule as a checklist of AND conditions. Steps: (1) test every main condition; if all pass → the positive action. (2) If a condition fails, check whether a stated EXCEPTION covers that exact failure — exceptions usually redirect ('refer to…') rather than auto-select. (3) If a required piece of data is missing, the answer is 'data inadequate'. (4) Apply only the given rules; do not use outside judgement. Work condition-by-condition and stop at the first decisive outcome.

patterns.name: All-conditions AND
patterns.howItWorks: Every main criterion must hold for selection.

patterns.name: Exception trigger
patterns.whatItLooksLike: fail a rule → check its exception
patterns.howItWorks: Apply an exception only if it names that exact shortfall.
patterns.example: Age short but top scorer → refer to Director.

patterns.name: Missing data
patterns.howItWorks: Cannot decide without a needed value.

patterns.name: No outside rules
patterns.howItWorks: Ignore personal opinion or real-world norms.

patterns.name: First decisive stop
patterns.howItWorks: Once a rule forces a decision, no need to check further.
patterns.example: Fails a must-have with no exception → reject.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — All conditions met
workedTypes.idea: Straight selection. What makes it different: every rule passes.
workedTypes.problem: Rule: Select if graduate AND age ≥ 21. Candidate: graduate, age 23. Decision?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — One condition fails
workedTypes.idea: Reject on a must-have. What makes it different: no exception.
workedTypes.problem: Rule: Select if graduate AND age ≥ 21 (no exceptions). Candidate: graduate, age 19. Decision?

steps.action: Age 19 < 21 fails, and no exception exists.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Exception applies
workedTypes.idea: Redirect, not select. What makes it different: matched exception.
workedTypes.problem: Rule: Select if graduate AND age ≥ 21; but if age < 21 and candidate scored above 90%, refer to the Director. Candidate: graduate, age 20, 94%. Decision?

steps.action: Age fails, but the 'age<21 and >90%' exception matches.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Data inadequate
workedTypes.idea: Missing field. What makes it different: cannot decide.
workedTypes.problem: Rule: Select if graduate AND age ≥ 21. Candidate: graduate, age not stated. Decision?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Multiple rules
workedTypes.idea: Check each in order. What makes it different: several criteria.
workedTypes.problem: Rule: Select if graduate AND age 21–30 AND passed the interview. Candidate: graduate, age 28, failed interview. Decision?

traps: Selecting when only some AND conditions are met.
Applying an exception that doesn't match the exact failure.
Using outside judgement instead of the given rules.
Deciding when a required piece of data is actually missing.
Continuing to second-guess after a decisive condition already fixes the outcome.
understand.memoryHook: Check every AND condition; if one fails, look for a matching exception (which usually 'refers', not selects); missing required data → data inadequate.

crack.oneLine: Run each rule as an AND checklist: all pass → select; a must-have fails with no matching exception → reject; a failure that matches a stated exception → follow its redirect; a missing required field → data inadequate.

techniques.name: Checklist
techniques.method: Tick each main condition in turn.

techniques.name: Exception match
techniques.method: Only fire an exception naming that exact shortfall.

techniques.name: Missing-data flag
techniques.method: If a required value is absent, answer 'data inadequate'.
techniques.howToApply: Check each rule has its input.
techniques.whenFails: When the field isn't actually required.

techniques.name: First-decisive stop
techniques.method: Stop once a condition fixes the outcome.

crack.whyItWorks: Eligibility rules are boolean AND conditions with explicit exception branches. Evaluating them literally and only firing named exceptions reproduces the intended policy exactly, so the correct decision is deterministic.
bestFor: Structured eligibility/selection rule sets.
Cases with clear main criteria and stated exceptions.
notFor: Ambiguous or incomplete rule sets can't be decided mechanically.
Never substitute personal judgement for a missing rule.
crack.practicePattern: Every decision item: checklist the conditions, fire only matching exceptions, flag missing data, and stop at the first decisive outcome.

topics.description: Decide if the clues are enough to reach a definite answer.

understand.realLife: Imagine a friend asks 'Is Ravi taller than Sam?' and gives you two hints. Sometimes one hint alone answers it, sometimes you need both, sometimes even both aren't enough. Data-sufficiency questions test whether the clues are ENOUGH to answer — not what the actual answer is.
understand.coreIdea: You get a question plus two statements (I and II). You must decide which statements are sufficient to answer definitely — WITHOUT fully solving. The standard five choices are: (A) I alone is sufficient, (B) II alone is sufficient, (C) both together are needed, (D) each alone is sufficient, (E) even both together are not sufficient. Here we use four options per question for a clean quiz, but the logic is identical.
understand.howToThink: Golden rule: judge SUFFICIENCY, not the answer. Test each statement in isolation first. A statement is sufficient if it pins the answer to ONE definite value (or a definite yes/no). Steps: (1) Does I alone decide it? (2) Does II alone decide it? (3) If neither alone, do they decide it together? (4) If not even together, it's insufficient. Beware: a statement giving a range or two possible answers is NOT sufficient. Never carry information from statement I into judging statement II.

patterns.name: Sufficiency, not solving
patterns.howItWorks: You only need to know it CAN be answered.

patterns.name: Test each alone
patterns.whatItLooksLike: judge I and II separately first
patterns.howItWorks: Don't let one statement leak into the other.
patterns.example: Reset knowledge between I and II.

patterns.name: Together only if needed
patterns.whatItLooksLike: combine only when neither alone works
patterns.howItWorks: Use both statements just when singles fail.
patterns.example: I gives sum, II gives difference → together.

patterns.name: Range ≠ sufficient
patterns.howItWorks: A statement must fix a single answer.

patterns.name: Yes/No definiteness
patterns.whatItLooksLike: a firm yes OR firm no ⇒ sufficient
patterns.howItWorks: Even a definite 'no' counts as answered.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — One alone sufficient
workedTypes.idea: Single statement decides. What makes it different: the other is extra.
workedTypes.problem: Q: What is Ravi's age? I. Ravi is 25. II. Ravi is older than Sam.

steps.action: I gives the exact age; II only compares.
steps.why: I fixes one value, II gives a range.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Both together needed
workedTypes.idea: Neither alone, both do. What makes it different: complementary clues.
workedTypes.problem: Q: Find two numbers. I. Their sum is 10. II. Their difference is 4.

steps.action: I alone: many pairs; II alone: many pairs.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Each alone sufficient
workedTypes.idea: Either works. What makes it different: both fix the answer.
workedTypes.problem: Q: Is x positive? I. x = 5. II. x² = 25 and x > 0.

steps.action: I gives x=5 (positive); II also forces x=5.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Not sufficient even together
workedTypes.idea: Still ambiguous. What makes it different: gap remains.
workedTypes.problem: Q: What is x? I. x is a positive integer. II. x < 10.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Definite 'no' counts
workedTypes.idea: A firm no answers it. What makes it different: yes/no question.
workedTypes.problem: Q: Is n an even number? I. n = 13.

steps.action: n = 13 is odd, so the answer is a definite 'no'.
steps.why: A firm no is still an answer.

traps: Actually solving the problem instead of judging sufficiency.
Letting statement I influence your reading of statement II.
Treating a range or multiple answers as sufficient.
Assuming 'both together' before testing each alone.
Thinking a definite 'no' means insufficient — it is sufficient.
understand.memoryHook: Ask 'can I get ONE definite answer?' — test I alone, then II alone (fresh), then both. A range is never sufficient; a firm yes OR no is.

crack.oneLine: Judge sufficiency, not the answer: test I alone, then II alone (reset in between), then both together only if needed — one definite value (or firm yes/no) means sufficient; a range never is.

techniques.name: Alone-first
techniques.method: Check each statement independently before combining.

techniques.name: One-value test
techniques.method: Sufficient only if the answer is pinned to a single value/verdict.

techniques.name: Combine last
techniques.method: Use both only when singles fail.

techniques.name: No/verdict rule
techniques.method: A definite yes OR no answers a yes/no question.

clueToMethod.clue: one statement gives an exact value
clueToMethod.method: That one alone is likely sufficient.

clueToMethod.clue: both give the same exact answer
yes/no question with a firm verdict

drill.question: Do you solve or judge sufficiency?
Test II using info from I?

crack.whyItWorks: Sufficiency is about whether the clues constrain the answer to a unique outcome. Testing each statement in isolation and only combining when necessary maps directly to the five standard DS choices, so the verdict is exact.
bestFor: Standard two-statement data-sufficiency questions.
Both value-type and yes/no questions.
notFor: If statements are contradictory, the question is flawed.
Do not overthink — you never need the final numeric answer.
crack.practicePattern: Every DS item: test I alone, reset, test II alone, then both — pick the choice matching where a single definite answer first appears.

groups.description: Picture shapes in your head — cubes, folded paper, mirror images and rotations.

topics.description: Painted and cut cube problems — count faces and pieces.

understand.realLife: Imagine painting a big wooden cube on all sides, then sawing it into lots of small equal cubes. Some small cubes end up painted on 3 sides (the corners), some on 2, some on 1, and some not at all (hidden inside). Counting these is a favourite exam puzzle — and it follows fixed formulas, so no drawing is needed.
understand.coreIdea: A cube with edge n is painted on all 6 outer faces and cut into n³ small cubes. You are asked how many small cubes have 3, 2, 1 or 0 painted faces. Because a cube always has 8 corners, 12 edges and 6 faces, these counts are given by simple formulas in n. Learn the four formulas and every painted-cube question becomes plug-and-play.
understand.howToThink: Cut a cube of edge n into n³ unit cubes. 3 painted faces = corner cubes = always 8. 2 painted faces = edge cubes (not corners) = 12(n−2). 1 painted face = face-centre cubes = 6(n−2)². 0 painted faces = fully hidden inside = (n−2)³. Check: 8 + 12(n−2) + 6(n−2)² + (n−2)³ = n³. For a cube painted on only some faces or with two colours, adjust which faces count, but the same corner/edge/face structure applies.

patterns.name: Total small cubes
patterns.howItWorks: Edge n cut into n slices each way.

patterns.name: 3 painted faces (corners)
patterns.howItWorks: A cube has exactly 8 corners regardless of n.

patterns.name: 2 painted faces (edges)
patterns.howItWorks: 12 edges, each with (n−2) middle cubes.

patterns.name: 1 painted face
patterns.howItWorks: 6 faces, each with an (n−2)² inner square.

patterns.name: 0 painted faces (inside)
patterns.howItWorks: The hidden inner cube of edge (n−2).

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Total pieces
workedTypes.idea: Cube the edge. What makes it different: just n³.
workedTypes.problem: A cube of edge 4 is cut into unit cubes. How many small cubes result?

steps.why: Cut into n pieces per side.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Three painted faces
workedTypes.idea: Always 8. What makes it different: corners are fixed.
workedTypes.problem: A painted cube of edge 5 is cut into unit cubes. How many have exactly 3 painted faces?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Two painted faces
workedTypes.idea: Edges formula. What makes it different: 12(n−2).
workedTypes.problem: A painted cube of edge 5 is cut. How many small cubes have exactly 2 painted faces?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — One painted face
workedTypes.idea: Face formula. What makes it different: 6(n−2)².
workedTypes.problem: A painted cube of edge 5 is cut. How many have exactly 1 painted face?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — No painted face
workedTypes.idea: Inner cube. What makes it different: (n−2)³.
workedTypes.problem: A painted cube of edge 5 is cut. How many small cubes have no painted face?

traps: Thinking corner (3-face) cubes depend on n — they are always 8.
Using n instead of (n−2) in the edge/face/inside formulas.
Squaring or cubing the wrong quantity (use (n−2), not n).
Forgetting the totals must add up to n³ (a quick check).
Applying all-faces formulas when only some faces are painted.
understand.memoryHook: Corners = 8, edges = 12(n−2), faces = 6(n−2)², inside = (n−2)³. They sum to n³ — use that as your check.

crack.oneLine: For a painted cube of edge n cut into unit cubes: 3-faces = 8, 2-faces = 12(n−2), 1-face = 6(n−2)², 0-faces = (n−2)³ — and all four add to n³.

techniques.name: Corner constant
techniques.method: 3-painted = 8 always.

techniques.name: Edge line
techniques.method: 2-painted = 12(n−2).

techniques.name: Face square
techniques.method: 1-painted = 6(n−2)².

techniques.name: Inside cube
techniques.method: 0-painted = (n−2)³.

crack.whyItWorks: A cube's surface always has 8 corners (3 faces meet), 12 edges (2 faces meet) and 6 face-interiors (1 face), while the untouched core is a cube of edge (n−2). Counting the unit cubes in each region gives the fixed formulas.
bestFor: Fully painted cubes cut into equal unit cubes.
Any edge length n ≥ 2.
notFor: If only some faces are painted, recount which corners/edges are affected.
Non-cubical blocks (cuboids) need per-dimension counting.
crack.practicePattern: Every painted-cube question: identify how many faces are asked, then apply 8 / 12(n−2) / 6(n−2)² / (n−2)³.

topics.description: Predict how folded and cut paper looks when opened.

understand.realLife: As a kid you probably folded paper, snipped a shape, and opened it to find a symmetric pattern of holes. Paper-folding questions test exactly that: given how paper is folded and where it is cut, predict the opened-out pattern. The secret is symmetry — every fold mirrors the cut.
understand.coreIdea: A sheet is folded one or more times, then a hole or notch is cut. When unfolded, each fold reflects the cut across the fold line, so the number of holes doubles with every fold (if the cut misses the fold lines). You reason about how many holes appear and where, using mirror symmetry about each crease.
understand.howToThink: Key rule: each fold is a mirror line. A single cut hole becomes 2 after unfolding one fold, 4 after two folds, 8 after three — i.e. holes = (cut count) × 2^(folds), provided the cut doesn't lie on a fold line (a cut on the crease is shared, not doubled). Positions: reflect the hole across the last fold first, then the previous fold, and so on back to the flat sheet. Symmetry about each crease gives the final arrangement.

patterns.name: Doubling rule
patterns.howItWorks: c cuts, f folds; each fold mirrors every hole.

patterns.name: Fold = mirror line
patterns.whatItLooksLike: reflect the cut across each crease
patterns.howItWorks: Unfolding mirrors the cut back over each fold.
patterns.example: Hole near right edge → mirrored to left.

patterns.name: Cut on the crease
patterns.howItWorks: A cut exactly on a fold line counts once across it.
patterns.example: Half-circle on the fold → one full circle.

patterns.name: Unfold order
patterns.howItWorks: Undo the last fold first when placing holes.

patterns.name: Symmetry
patterns.whatItLooksLike: final pattern is symmetric about every crease
patterns.howItWorks: The opened sheet has mirror symmetry along each fold used.
patterns.example: Two folds → symmetric across both lines.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — One fold, one hole
workedTypes.idea: Doubles to two. What makes it different: single mirror.
workedTypes.problem: A square sheet is folded once (in half) and a single hole is punched (not on the fold). How many holes appear when unfolded?

steps.why: One fold mirrors the hole once.

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Two folds, one hole
workedTypes.idea: Doubles twice. What makes it different: 2² = 4.
workedTypes.problem: A sheet is folded twice and one hole is punched (off the folds). How many holes on unfolding?

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Cut on the fold
workedTypes.idea: Shared, not doubled. What makes it different: on-crease cut.
workedTypes.problem: A sheet is folded once and a half-circle is cut with its straight edge exactly on the fold. What shape appears when unfolded?

steps.action: The half-circle mirrors across the fold to complete a full circle.
steps.why: The cut lies on the mirror line.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Three folds
workedTypes.idea: Doubles thrice. What makes it different: 2³ = 8.
workedTypes.problem: A sheet is folded three times and one hole is punched (off the folds). How many holes result?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two holes, two folds
workedTypes.idea: Scale the count. What makes it different: multiple cuts.
workedTypes.problem: A sheet is folded twice and two separate holes are punched (off the folds). How many holes on unfolding?

traps: Forgetting that each fold DOUBLES the holes.
Doubling a cut that lies exactly on a fold line (it's shared, not doubled).
Unfolding in the wrong order (undo the last fold first).
Ignoring the mirror symmetry when placing holes.
Confusing folds with cuts when counting.
understand.memoryHook: Holes = cuts × 2^folds (unless the cut sits on a crease). Each fold is a mirror; unfold last-fold-first.

crack.oneLine: Each fold is a mirror line, so holes = cuts × 2^(folds) — unless a cut lies exactly on a crease (then it's shared, not doubled) — and the opened sheet is symmetric about every fold.

techniques.name: Power of two
techniques.method: Multiply cuts by 2 for each fold.

techniques.name: Crease share
techniques.method: A cut on the fold counts once across it.

techniques.name: Mirror placement
techniques.method: Reflect the hole across each crease to place it.

techniques.name: Symmetry check
techniques.method: Final pattern must be symmetric about each fold.

crack.whyItWorks: Unfolding reverses each fold, and a fold is a reflection, so every cut is copied to its mirror image across each crease. With f independent folds a single cut has 2^f mirror images, giving the doubling rule.
bestFor: Standard straight folds with cuts away from the creases.
Counting the number of holes/shapes on unfolding.
notFor: Cuts lying on a fold line are shared, not doubled.
Diagonal or overlapping folds need careful case-by-case mirroring.
crack.practicePattern: Every paper-fold question: count folds, apply cuts × 2^folds (watch on-crease cuts), then mirror to place the holes.

topics.description: Find how a figure looks reflected in a mirror or in water.

understand.realLife: Hold a word up to a mirror and it reads backwards; look at a reflection in still water and it flips upside down. Mirror- and water-image questions ask you to predict these reflections for letters, numbers and figures — a test of how well you flip an image in your head.
understand.coreIdea: A MIRROR image is a left–right flip (reflection about a vertical line) — as if a mirror stands to the side. A WATER image is a top–bottom flip (reflection about a horizontal line) — as if water lies below. You decide how a given letter, number, word or shape looks after each kind of flip. Some symmetric characters stay unchanged; the rest reverse.
understand.howToThink: Mirror image = reflect left↔right: the order of letters reverses AND each letter is flipped horizontally. Letters symmetric about a vertical axis (A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y) look the SAME in a mirror; others (B, C, D, E, etc.) change. Water image = reflect top↔bottom: letters symmetric about a horizontal axis (B, C, D, E, H, I, K, O, X) look the same; others flip. For words: a mirror reverses the whole sequence (READ → DAER reading right-to-left), while water keeps the order but flips each letter vertically. Numbers follow the same idea (e.g. 0, 1, 8 are vertically symmetric).

patterns.name: Mirror = left–right flip
patterns.howItWorks: Letter order reverses; each glyph flips horizontally.

patterns.name: Water = top–bottom flip
patterns.howItWorks: Order kept; each glyph flips vertically.

patterns.name: Vertical-symmetry letters
patterns.whatItLooksLike: A H I M O T U V W X Y unchanged in mirror
patterns.howItWorks: These read the same reflected left–right.

patterns.name: Horizontal-symmetry letters
patterns.whatItLooksLike: B C D E H I K O X unchanged in water
patterns.howItWorks: These read the same reflected top–bottom.

patterns.name: Word order rule
patterns.whatItLooksLike: mirror reverses order; water keeps order
patterns.howItWorks: Mirror flips the sequence; water only flips each glyph.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Mirror of a word
workedTypes.idea: Reverse the order. What makes it different: left–right flip.
workedTypes.problem: What is the mirror image of the word 'CODE' (mirror on the right side)?

steps.action: Reverse the letter order: E D O C.
Reading the reflection gives the reversed sequence 'EDOC' (with each letter flipped).

workedTypes.answer: The letters appear in reverse order (E, D, O, C).

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Mirror-invariant letters
workedTypes.idea: Some look the same. What makes it different: vertical symmetry.
workedTypes.problem: Which of these letters looks unchanged in a mirror: A, B, H, E?

steps.action: A and H are symmetric about a vertical line; B and E are not.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Water image of a letter
workedTypes.idea: Top–bottom flip. What makes it different: horizontal reflection.
workedTypes.problem: Which of these letters looks unchanged in its water image: E, C, A, F?

steps.action: E and C are symmetric about a horizontal line; A and F are not.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Mirror of a number
workedTypes.idea: Digits flip too. What makes it different: numeric glyphs.
workedTypes.problem: In a mirror, which digits among 0, 1, 8, 3 look unchanged?

steps.action: 0, 1 and 8 are vertically symmetric; 3 is not.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Mirror vs water difference
workedTypes.idea: Pick the right flip. What makes it different: compare both.
workedTypes.problem: For the letter 'b', what is its mirror image and its water image (by shape)?

traps: Swapping the two: mirror is left–right, water is top–bottom.
Forgetting a mirror also reverses the ORDER of letters in a word.
Assuming all letters change — symmetric ones stay the same.
Mixing up which letters are vertically vs horizontally symmetric.
Flipping order in a water image (water keeps order, only flips each glyph).
understand.memoryHook: Mirror = side flip (order reverses); Water = down flip (order stays). Vertical-symmetry letters (A H I M O T U V W X Y) survive a mirror; horizontal-symmetry (B C D E H I K O X) survive water.

crack.oneLine: Mirror image = left–right flip that reverses letter order; water image = top–bottom flip that keeps order — and symmetric letters (vertical set for mirror, horizontal set for water) stay unchanged.

techniques.name: Flip direction
techniques.method: Mirror = vertical line (side), water = horizontal line (below).

techniques.name: Order rule
techniques.method: Mirror reverses word order; water doesn't.

techniques.name: Symmetry set
techniques.method: Vertical set survives mirror; horizontal set survives water.

techniques.name: Digit check
techniques.method: 0,1,8 vertically symmetric; 0,1,3,8 horizontally symmetric.

clueToMethod.method: Apply the same symmetry to digits.

crack.whyItWorks: A mirror reflects across a vertical axis and water across a horizontal axis. Letters whose shape is symmetric about that axis map onto themselves; a word's reading direction reverses only for the side-mirror, which is why order flips for mirror but not water.
bestFor: Letter, number and word reflection questions.
Deciding which characters remain unchanged.
notFor: Highly stylised or lowercase fonts can change which letters are symmetric.
Angled/clock-based mirror questions need the specific mirror line.
crack.practicePattern: Every reflection question: pick mirror (side) or water (down), flip about that axis, reverse order only for mirror, and use the symmetry sets for unchanged characters.

topics.description: Odd figures, embedded figures and completing a figure pattern.

understand.realLife: Spot-the-difference games, 'which one doesn't belong', and 'find the hidden shape' puzzles are everywhere — from kids' magazines to design tests. Visual reasoning questions package these into odd-one-out figures, embedded (hidden) figures, and complete-the-pattern grids. They reward a sharp eye and a systematic checklist.
understand.coreIdea: You compare figures on a few features — number of sides/lines, shading, rotation, symmetry, count of elements — and either pick the one that breaks the rule (odd one out), find a simpler shape hidden inside a complex one (embedded), or choose the piece that completes a pattern. Since these are visual, we describe each figure in words and reason about the same features.
understand.howToThink: Work through a fixed feature checklist: (1) count elements (lines, dots, sides); (2) shading/colour; (3) orientation/rotation; (4) symmetry; (5) open vs closed shapes; (6) added/removed parts. Odd-one-out: four share a feature, one differs — find the differing feature. Embedded figure: the target must appear with the SAME size, orientation and proportions inside the bigger figure, without rotating or resizing. Complete-the-pattern: find the transformation rule across a row/column (rotate by a fixed angle, add one element, shade the next region) and apply it to the blank.

patterns.name: Feature checklist
patterns.howItWorks: Scan the same features across all options.
patterns.example: Four have 4 lines, one has 5 → odd.

patterns.name: Odd one out
patterns.whatItLooksLike: the figure breaking the shared rule
patterns.howItWorks: Four match on a feature; one doesn't.

patterns.name: Embedded figure
patterns.howItWorks: Target sits in the big figure without rotating/resizing.
patterns.example: A triangle traced by existing lines.

patterns.name: Series/analogy rule
patterns.howItWorks: Fixed rotation or element-change across the sequence.

patterns.name: Element count
patterns.howItWorks: Often the distinguishing feature.
patterns.example: Odd figure has one extra dot.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Odd one out (count)
workedTypes.idea: Compare a number. What makes it different: element count.
workedTypes.problem: Four figures are closed shapes with 4 sides each (square, rectangle, rhombus, trapezium); the fifth is a triangle (3 sides). Which is the odd one out?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Odd one out (symmetry)
workedTypes.idea: Compare symmetry. What makes it different: a non-symmetric figure.
workedTypes.problem: Among figures A (square), B (circle), C (equilateral triangle), D (scalene triangle), which lacks the symmetry the others have?

steps.action: A, B, C are symmetric; D is scalene (no equal sides).

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Embedded figure
workedTypes.idea: Hidden shape. What makes it different: same size & orientation.
workedTypes.problem: A complex figure is drawn from a big square crossed by both diagonals. Which simple shape is definitely embedded: a smaller triangle, a circle, a pentagon, or a hexagon?

steps.action: Diagonals split the square into 4 triangles.
A triangle is embedded; circle/pentagon/hexagon are not.

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Complete the pattern (rotation)
workedTypes.idea: Apply the rule. What makes it different: fixed rotation.
workedTypes.problem: In a series, an arrow points up, then right, then down. What comes next if it rotates 90° clockwise each step?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Complete the pattern (add element)
workedTypes.idea: Grow the count. What makes it different: add one each step.
workedTypes.problem: A figure shows 1 dot, then 2 dots, then 3 dots. If each step adds one dot, how many dots in the next figure?

traps: Fixating on one feature and missing the real distinguishing one.
Rotating or resizing when hunting an embedded figure (it must match exactly).
Assuming the odd one differs in shading when it actually differs in count.
Guessing the pattern rule instead of confirming it across all given figures.
Ignoring open vs closed shapes as a distinguishing feature.
understand.memoryHook: Run the checklist: Count, Colour, Rotation, Symmetry, Open/Closed. The feature that four share and one breaks is your answer.

crack.oneLine: Scan every option on a fixed feature checklist (count, shading, rotation, symmetry, open/closed) — the odd figure breaks the shared rule, an embedded figure must appear at the same size and orientation, and a pattern is solved by finding and applying the transform.

techniques.name: Checklist scan
techniques.method: Compare count → shading → rotation → symmetry.

techniques.name: Same-orientation rule
techniques.method: Embedded figure keeps size & angle.

techniques.name: Transform-then-apply
techniques.method: Find the step rule, apply to the blank.

techniques.name: Count first
techniques.method: Element count is often the differentiator.

crack.whyItWorks: Figure puzzles are built by varying a small set of features, so a disciplined scan of those same features reliably exposes the intended difference or rule, rather than relying on a lucky glance.
bestFor: Odd-one-out, embedded-figure and complete-the-pattern items.
Any set built on countable/rotational/symmetry features.
notFor: Ambiguous figures with more than one defensible rule.
Rotated-embedded variants where the target may be turned.
crack.practicePattern: Every visual-reasoning question: run the feature checklist across all options, then pick the breaker, the exactly-matching hidden shape, or the transform-completed figure.

topics.description: Rotate, fold and picture shapes in 2D and 3D.

understand.realLife: Packing a suitcase, reading a map while turning a corner, or folding a flat cardboard net into a box all use spatial reasoning — the ability to rotate and fold shapes in your mind. Exams test this with dice, cube-nets, rotations and 2D-to-3D folding.
understand.coreIdea: You mentally manipulate shapes: rotate a figure by a given angle, fold a flat 'net' into a solid (usually a cube or dice), and work out which faces end up opposite or adjacent. The classic tools are the dice-opposite rule and the cube-net opposite-faces rule. Presented in words here, the same logic applies as with a physical model.
understand.howToThink: Dice rule: opposite faces of a standard die sum to 7 (1-6, 2-5, 3-4). If two views of a die show the same face-up but different sides, compare to deduce which faces are opposite. Cube-net rule: on a cross/T shaped net, faces separated by exactly ONE face in a straight line are opposite; the two remaining pairs are also opposite. A cube has 3 pairs of opposite faces and each face is adjacent to the other 4. Rotation: turning a 2D shape by 90°/180°/270° cycles its orientation; 360° returns to start. For 'how many small cubes are visible', a cube of edge n shows 3 faces = fewer than n³ (hidden interior and back faces are not seen).

patterns.name: Standard die
patterns.howItWorks: 1-6, 2-5, 3-4 are opposite pairs.

patterns.name: Cube-net opposites
patterns.whatItLooksLike: one face apart in a straight line = opposite
patterns.howItWorks: Skip-one rule on the unfolded net.
patterns.example: Faces 1 and 3 in a row are opposite.

patterns.name: Opposite pairs
patterns.howItWorks: 6 faces = 3 pairs; each face adjacent to 4 others.

patterns.name: Rotation cycle
patterns.howItWorks: Four quarter-turns return to origin.

patterns.name: Adjacent count
patterns.howItWorks: Only the opposite face is non-adjacent.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Opposite face of a die
workedTypes.idea: Sum to 7. What makes it different: standard-die rule.
workedTypes.problem: On a standard die, which number is opposite to 3?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Cube net opposites
workedTypes.idea: Skip-one rule. What makes it different: unfolded net.
workedTypes.problem: A cube net has faces in a straight row labelled 1, 2, 3, 4. In the folded cube, which face is opposite face 2?

steps.action: In a straight line, a face is opposite the one two steps away.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Rotation
workedTypes.idea: Quarter-turn cycle. What makes it different: 270° = 90° the other way.
workedTypes.problem: An arrow points north. After rotating 270° clockwise, where does it point?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Adjacent faces
workedTypes.idea: All but opposite. What makes it different: adjacency count.
workedTypes.problem: On a standard die, to how many faces is the '1' face adjacent?

steps.action: Each face is adjacent to all except its opposite.

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Two-view die deduction
workedTypes.idea: Compare views. What makes it different: common face reasoning.
workedTypes.problem: Two views of a die: View 1 shows top 1, front 2, right 3. View 2 shows top 1, front 3, right 5. Which face is opposite 2?

steps.action: Top 1 is fixed; the die rotated about the vertical axis so 2→3→5→... cycle around.
steps.why: Side faces rotate around the fixed top.

steps.action: Going 2→3 (quarter turn), the face opposite 2 comes to front after a half turn = 5.

workedTypes.answer: 5 is opposite 2 (with 1 on top, 2 and 5 face-off across the die).

traps: Forgetting opposite die faces sum to 7.
Misapplying the net rule — count faces in a STRAIGHT line, skipping one.
Confusing clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation.
Assuming a face is adjacent to only some faces — it touches all but its opposite.
Not fixing a reference face when comparing two die views.
understand.memoryHook: Dice: opposites add to 7. Net: skip one in a line = opposite. Rotation: four 90° turns = back to start. Each face touches 4, opposes 1.

crack.oneLine: Use the die rule (opposite faces sum to 7), the cube-net skip-one rule for opposites, and quarter-turn cycles for rotations — remembering each face is adjacent to 4 others and opposite exactly 1.

techniques.name: Sum-to-7
techniques.method: Opposite die face = 7 − face.

techniques.name: Skip-one net
techniques.method: In a straight row, opposite = two steps away.

techniques.name: Quarter cycle
techniques.method: 270° CW = 90° CCW; 180° = reverse.

techniques.name: Adjacency 4
techniques.method: A face touches all but its opposite.

clueToMethod.method: Fix a face, track the rotation.

crack.whyItWorks: A cube's fixed geometry — 3 opposite pairs, each face adjacent to 4 — combined with the standard-die numbering (opposites sum to 7) turns spatial folding into arithmetic, so you rarely need to build the actual model.
bestFor: Standard dice, cube nets and quarter-turn rotations.
Opposite/adjacent face and simple 2D-rotation questions.
notFor: Non-standard dice or nets with unusual layouts.
Complex 3D rotations about a diagonal axis.
crack.practicePattern: Every spatial question: apply sum-to-7 for dice, skip-one for nets, quarter-turn reduction for rotations, and adjacency = 4.

groups.description: Solve angle and time problems on clocks, and find the day for any date.

topics.description: Find angles, overlaps and times on an analog clock.

understand.realLife: Every analog clock is a little maths machine: two hands sweep a 360° circle at fixed speeds. Once you know those speeds, you can find the exact angle between the hands at any time, when they overlap, when they point opposite, and even how much a fast or slow clock drifts. Exams love these because they reward one clean formula.
understand.coreIdea: The minute hand does a full 360° every 60 minutes, so it moves 6° per minute. The hour hand does 360° in 12 hours, so it moves 0.5° per minute (30° per hour). At time H:M the hands make an angle of |30H − 5.5M| degrees. From these facts everything else follows: how often the hands overlap, make 90°, or line up opposite each other.
understand.howToThink: Minute-hand speed = 6°/min; hour-hand speed = 0.5°/min; the minute hand gains 6 − 0.5 = 5.5° per minute on the hour hand. Angle at H:M = |30H − 5.5M| (take 360 − θ if it exceeds 180 to get the smaller angle). Overlaps: the hands coincide 11 times every 12 hours, i.e. every 720/11 ≈ 65 5/11 minutes. Right angles (90°): 22 times per 12 hours. Straight line/opposite (180°): 11 times per 12 hours. Fast/slow clock: if a clock gains or loses x minutes over a period, scale proportionally.

patterns.name: Angle between hands
patterns.howItWorks: Hour position 30H, minute position 5.5M relative; take smaller of θ and 360−θ.

patterns.name: Relative gain
patterns.howItWorks: Minute hand gains on hour hand.

patterns.name: Overlaps in 12 h
patterns.howItWorks: Hands coincide 11×, not 12.

patterns.name: Right angles / opposite in 12 h
patterns.howItWorks: 90° happens twice per hour on average; 180° once.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Angle at o'clock
workedTypes.idea: Plug M=0. What makes it different: minute hand at 12.
workedTypes.problem: What is the angle between the hands at 3:00?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Angle at H:M
workedTypes.idea: Use the formula. What makes it different: non-zero minutes.
workedTypes.problem: Find the angle between the hands at 3:30.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Straight line (180°)
workedTypes.idea: When opposite. What makes it different: angle = 180.
workedTypes.problem: At 6:00, what is the angle between the hands?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Overlaps count
workedTypes.idea: Hands coincide. What makes it different: 11 per 12 h.
workedTypes.problem: How many times do the hands of a clock overlap in 24 hours?

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Fast/slow clock
workedTypes.idea: Scale the drift. What makes it different: gain/loss rate.
workedTypes.problem: A clock gains 5 minutes every hour. How much does it gain in 12 hours?

traps: Forgetting the hour hand also moves as minutes pass (that's the 5.5M term).
Using 6M instead of 5.5M in the angle formula.
Giving the reflex angle instead of the smaller one (subtract from 360 if >180).
Assuming hands overlap 12 times in 12 hours — it is 11.
Mixing up the 90° count (22) and 180° count (11) per 12 hours.
understand.memoryHook: Angle = |30H − 5.5M|. Minute hand = 6°/min, hour hand = 0.5°/min, gap grows 5.5°/min. Overlaps 11×, right angles 22×, opposite 11× per 12 hours.

crack.oneLine: The angle between the hands at H:M is |30H − 5.5M| (take the smaller of θ and 360−θ), because the minute hand moves 6°/min and the hour hand 0.5°/min — and the hands overlap 11 times, make 90° 22 times and 180° 11 times every 12 hours.

techniques.name: Reflex fix
techniques.method: If θ > 180, use 360 − θ.

techniques.name: Overlap timing
techniques.method: Coincide every 720/11 ≈ 65 5/11 min.

techniques.name: Drift scaling
techniques.method: Gain/loss × time.

crack.whyItWorks: Both hands move at constant angular speeds, so their positions are linear in time: the minute hand at 6M° and the hour hand at (30H + 0.5M)°. Subtracting gives |30H − 5.5M|, and the fixed relative speed of 5.5°/min sets the rhythm of overlaps and special angles.
bestFor: Angle, overlap and special-angle questions on a standard 12-hour clock.
Uniform fast/slow clock drift.
notFor: Clocks with non-uniform errors need step-by-step tracking.
24-hour or unusual dials change the per-hour degree.
crack.practicePattern: Every clock question: apply |30H − 5.5M| for angles, fix the reflex, and use 11/22/11 counts for overlaps/right-angles/opposite.

topics.description: Find the day of the week for any date using odd days.

understand.realLife: 'What day of the week was 15 August 1947?' sounds impossible without a calendar, yet you can work it out in seconds using 'odd days'. The trick is that weeks repeat every 7 days, so only the REMAINDER of days matters. Master odd days and any date-to-day question becomes simple counting.
understand.coreIdea: An 'odd day' is the remainder left over after grouping days into complete weeks (days mod 7). An ordinary year has 365 days = 52 weeks + 1 odd day; a leap year has 366 = 52 weeks + 2 odd days. Counting odd days from a known reference date tells you how many weekdays to move forward, which gives the day of the week. Leap years (divisible by 4, but centuries only if divisible by 400) add the extra day in February.
understand.howToThink: Odd days = (total days) mod 7. Ordinary year → 1 odd day; leap year → 2 odd days. Century odd days: 100 yr = 5, 200 yr = 3, 300 yr = 1, 400 yr = 0 (and it repeats every 400). Leap-year test: divisible by 4 is leap, EXCEPT century years, which must be divisible by 400 (so 1900 is not leap, 2000 is). Month odd days (Jan..Dec): 3,0,3,2,3,2,3,3,2,3,2,3. Day codes: 0=Sunday, 1=Monday, ... 6=Saturday. Add up odd days from the reference and reduce mod 7 to read off the day. Same calendar repeats after a gap whose odd days total 0 (commonly 6, 11, or 28 years apart depending on leap positions).

patterns.name: Odd days
patterns.howItWorks: Remainder after full weeks.

patterns.name: Leap-year test
patterns.howItWorks: 1900 not leap; 2000 leap.

patterns.name: Century odd days
patterns.howItWorks: Repeats every 400 years.

patterns.name: Day codes
patterns.howItWorks: Reduce total odd days mod 7.

workedTypes.name: Type 1 — Odd days in a year
workedTypes.idea: Ordinary vs leap. What makes it different: year type.
workedTypes.problem: How many odd days are there in an ordinary (non-leap) year?

workedTypes.name: Type 2 — Leap year test
workedTypes.idea: Apply the rule. What makes it different: century exception.
workedTypes.problem: Is the year 1900 a leap year?

steps.action: 1900 ÷ 4 = 475, but it is a century year.
1900 ÷ 400 is not whole → NOT a leap year.

workedTypes.answer: No — 1900 is not a leap year.

workedTypes.name: Type 3 — Day after N days
workedTypes.idea: Count odd days. What makes it different: mod 7 shift.
workedTypes.problem: If today is Monday, what day will it be after 10 days?

workedTypes.name: Type 4 — Same day next year
workedTypes.idea: Shift by year odd days. What makes it different: +1 or +2.
workedTypes.problem: 1 Jan 2023 was a Sunday. What day is 1 Jan 2024? (2023 is an ordinary year.)

workedTypes.name: Type 5 — Century odd days
workedTypes.idea: Use the century value. What makes it different: 400-year cycle.
workedTypes.problem: How many odd days are there in 400 years?

traps: Treating every century year as a leap year (only those divisible by 400 are).
Counting all the days instead of just the remainder (odd days).
Forgetting a leap year contributes 2 odd days, not 1.
Shifting backward instead of forward when moving to a later date.
Mismatching the day code (0 = Sunday, not Monday).
understand.memoryHook: Only the remainder (days mod 7) matters. Ordinary year = 1 odd day, leap = 2. Century: 100→5, 200→3, 300→1, 400→0. Leap = ÷4, centuries need ÷400.

crack.oneLine: Convert everything to 'odd days' (days mod 7): an ordinary year adds 1, a leap year adds 2, centuries add 5/3/1/0 for 100/200/300/400 — then shift the known day forward by the total odd days to read off the answer.

techniques.name: Mod-7 only
techniques.method: Reduce day counts mod 7 first.

techniques.name: Year jump
techniques.method: +1 per ordinary year, +2 per leap year.
techniques.whenFails: Crossing a leap boundary — count the leap day.

techniques.name: Leap test
techniques.method: ÷4 leap; century must be ÷400.

drill.question: Odd days in an ordinary year?
Odd days in a leap year?

crack.whyItWorks: Weekdays cycle every 7 days, so two dates fall on the same weekday exactly when the number of days between them is a multiple of 7. Working mod 7 (odd days) collapses huge day counts into a shift of 0–6, which directly names the weekday.
bestFor: Day-of-week, leap-year and same-calendar questions in the Gregorian calendar.
Any date-to-date weekday shift.
notFor: Non-Gregorian calendars use different leap rules.
Very old dates before the Gregorian reform need adjustment.
crack.practicePattern: Every calendar question: total the odd days (years, months, leap days, centuries), reduce mod 7, and shift the reference day forward.
